title: Make BullMQ email worker idempotent to stop duplicate order confirmations after retries
business_context: A Shopify-competitor checkout service sends order-confirmation emails via a BullMQ worker. During a SendGrid 30s outage, jobs timed out after the email was actually accepted, BullMQ retried them, and 12,400 customers received 2-4 duplicate confirmations in one afternoon. Support tickets spiked 6x and the brand looked broken.
constraints: Worker runs 8 replicas on Kubernetes, each with concurrency 20 (160 parallel jobs)
Must remain backward-compatible with already-enqueued jobs lacking an idempotency key
SendGrid p99 latency is 4s; job attempt timeout is 15s
Redis is shared across 30 queues, so no per-job Lua scripts that scan keyspace
At-least-once delivery is acceptable, duplicate sends are not

before_state.description: The worker sends the email then marks success. On any retry it re-sends because there is no dedupe; the SendGrid call is not guarded.

issues: No idempotency: a retry after a successful send re-delivers the email
attempt timeout can fire after SendGrid already accepted the message
No record that orderId+template was already sent
No deterministic jobId so duplicate enqueues also create duplicate jobs
No dedupe window
Retries inherit the same un-guarded handler

reasoning: Naive fix: lower attempts to 1 so retries never happen — rejected because then a genuine transient SendGrid 503 permanently loses the email, which is worse than a duplicate. Alternative: wrap send in a DB transaction that inserts a row first — rejected as the sole mechanism because the email send is a non-transactional external side effect; a crash between insert and send loses mail. Chosen path is a two-phase idempotency record with a SETNX claim plus a durable sent_emails table. Cross-layer trace: API enqueue sets a deterministic jobId order:{orderId}:confirm so duplicate enqueues collapse (config); the worker first does Redis SET key NX with a 24h TTL to claim the send; if the claim is lost it checks sent_emails for a committed row and short-circuits; after SendGrid returns a messageId it writes sent_emails(order_id, template, message_id) (data); tests assert single send under 5 forced retries and a chaos test kills the worker between claim and send to prove recovery; rollout flags the dedupe path per queue.

after_state.description: Sends are guarded by a Redis NX claim and a durable sent_emails ledger, so retries and duplicate enqueues send exactly once.

files.purpose: Guards each send behind a Redis NX claim and a durable ledger so retries/duplicate enqueues are exactly-once
Deterministic jobId collapses duplicate enqueues into one job

files.content:
CREATE TABLE sent_emails (
  order_id    text NOT NULL,
  template    text NOT NULL,
  message_id  text NOT NULL,
  sent_at     timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (order_id, template)
);
files.purpose: Durable exactly-once ledger; primary key enforces one send per order+template even if Redis is flushed
Asserts exactly-once send across a simulated retry

migration_strategy: Ship sent_emails migration and deploy worker code reading a DEDUP_EMAILS=false flag (no behavior change)
Backfill: deterministic jobId only applies to new enqueues; old random-id jobs still drain through the guarded handler safely
Enable DEDUP_EMAILS=true for 5% of orders via flag and watch duplicate-rate metric drop
Ramp to 100% over 3 days while monitoring sent_emails write errors
Remove the flag and delete the legacy un-guarded code path
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds an order and runs the handler twice with the same jobId; assert SendGrid.send called once AND exactly one sent_emails row AND the second call returns dedup:true. Side-effect asserts on both Redis key presence and DB row. Chaos test: mock SendGrid to resolve but kill the process before the DB write, restart, re-run the job, and assert the ledger row is created and no second email is sent because the NX claim plus ledger re-check short-circuits.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With attempts>1 and no idempotency, any attempt timeout that fires after SendGrid accepted the message causes BullMQ to re-run the handler and re-send, multiplying emails by the number of retries under exactly the outage conditions retries exist to handle.

title: Add a dead-letter queue with replay tooling for permanently failing Kafka payment-settlement events
business_context: A fintech settlement service consumes payment-captured events from Kafka and posts ledger entries. When a downstream ledger schema migration rejected 3 event versions, those events failed forever, the consumer kept retrying them in-place, and partition lag grew to 4.2M messages, stalling settlement for all merchants for 90 minutes.
constraints: Kafka topic has 24 partitions; consumer group has 12 instances
Ledger API is exactly-once via an idempotency key; no double-posting allowed
Must preserve event ordering per merchantId within a partition
Poison messages must not block the partition for healthy messages
Replay must be auditable for SOC2 (who replayed, when, why)

before_state.description: The consumer commits offsets only after success and retries the same record in a tight loop, head-of-line blocking the whole partition on one poison message.

issues: A single unprocessable event blocks every later event in the partition
No DLQ, so retries are infinite and lag explodes
No idempotency key passed to ledger.post

reasoning: Naive fix: catch-and-skip the bad event so the partition keeps moving — rejected because silently dropping settlement events loses money and fails audit. Alternative: pause/resume the partition on failure — rejected because it still head-of-line blocks and merely converts an infinite retry into an infinite pause. Chosen path: bounded in-place retry then route the poison message to a dlq.settlement topic with full headers, commit the offset, and provide an audited replay CLI. Cross-layer trace: consumer parses and posts with merchantId+eventId idempotency key (data); on failure it retries N times with jitter, then produces to DLQ with original partition/offset/error headers and commits (async); a replay service reads DLQ, optionally transforms, and re-produces to the main topic with a replay-audit row (data+config); tests cover poison-routing and ordered replay; rollout dual-runs old and new consumers behind a group rename.

after_state.description: Bounded retries then DLQ routing keep partitions flowing; an audited replay tool reprocesses fixed events.

files.purpose: Bounded retry then DLQ route so a poison message never blocks the partition
Audited, filtered replay that re-produces fixed events to the original topic

files.content:
CREATE TABLE replay_audit (
  id        bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  actor     text NOT NULL,
  reason    text NOT NULL,
  event_key text NOT NULL,
  at        timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
files.purpose: SOC2 audit trail of who replayed what and why

migration_strategy: Create dlq.settlement topic with 24 partitions and the replay_audit table
Deploy consumer as new group settlement-v2 reading from the committed offsets of settlement, run in shadow on 2 partitions
Cut over consumer group to v2; old group settlement is left paused for instant rollback
Drain backlog; verify DLQ receives only genuinely-poison events
Run replay for the 3 known bad event versions after the ledger schema is fixed, capturing audit rows
testing_strategy: Fixture produces a mix of valid events and 1 schema-invalid event into a test topic. Assert valid events post to ledger (output) and that the invalid event lands in dlq.settlement with origOffset header (side-effect), and the consumer offset advanced past it. Chaos test: make ledger.post fail transiently 2 times then succeed and assert the event is NOT sent to DLQ (retry recovered) and is posted exactly once via idempotency key.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throwing forever on one unprocessable record at the eachMessage boundary means kafkajs never commits past it, so that partition stops, and because the schema migration broke 3 event versions spread across partitions, settlement halts merchant-wide rather than for the few bad events.

title: Add backpressure to a Node image-resize worker to stop OOM kills under viral upload spikes
business_context: A social photo app resizes uploads with a Sharp-based worker. When a creator with 8M followers posted, uploads spiked to 5,000/min, the worker pulled all jobs into memory at once, RSS hit the 2GB container limit, and Kubernetes OOM-killed the pod 40 times in 20 minutes, dropping in-flight resizes and showing broken thumbnails.
constraints: Container memory limit is 2GB; each Sharp resize peaks ~180MB
Worker autoscales 3-20 replicas on queue depth
Must not lose jobs on OOM/restart (at-least-once)
Original images are 4000x4000 up to 25MB pulled from S3
Thumbnail SLA is p95 under 30s end-to-end

before_state.description: Worker uses unbounded concurrency and prefetches the whole waiting set, so memory scales with queue depth and the pod OOMs.

issues: No concurrency limit so N jobs load N originals into RAM simultaneously
No backpressure tied to actual memory headroom
Large originals decoded fully before resize, spiking RSS

reasoning: Naive fix: set concurrency to a small constant like 4 — rejected alone because original sizes vary 10x so a fixed count still OOMs on a batch of 25MB images while underutilizing on small ones. Alternative: vertically scale memory to 8GB — rejected as it just raises the cliff and triples cost without fixing the unbounded behavior. Chosen path: bound concurrency, gate each job on a memory-headroom check, and stream/limit Sharp pixel budget. Cross-layer trace: queue stays the same; worker sets concurrency from a computed safe value, each job awaits a semaphore that also checks process.memoryUsage().rss against a high-water mark before decoding (async); Sharp uses limitInputPixels and sequentialRead to cap decode memory (data); autoscaler scales on depth while concurrency caps per-pod memory (config); tests assert RSS stays under limit under a burst and a chaos test feeds oversized images.

after_state.description: Concurrency is bounded and memory-gated; Sharp is constrained so RSS stays well under the 2GB limit even during bursts.

files.purpose: Bounds concurrency and gates each decode on memory headroom; constrains Sharp decode size
Semaphore plus live RSS check that throttles decoding when memory is tight
Asserts peak RSS stays under the container limit during a burst

migration_strategy: Deploy MemoryGate with maxInFlight set high (20) so behavior is unchanged, behind METRIC_ONLY mode logging would-be throttles
Lower maxInFlight to 6 and enable gating on 1 canary pod, compare OOM count to baseline
Roll gating to all pods; keep autoscaling on queue depth
Tune highWaterBytes from observed RSS histograms
Remove METRIC_ONLY mode once OOM kills are zero for a week
testing_strategy: Fixture enqueues 200 jobs referencing 25MB originals from a local MinIO. Assert all thumbnails written to S3 (output) and that sampled peak RSS < 1.9GB (side-effect). Chaos test: feed a 26000x26000 image; assert it is rejected by limitInputPixels rather than decoded, the job fails cleanly to BullMQ retry, and RSS does not spike.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With unbounded concurrency the worker decodes as many originals as the queue offers simultaneously; during the 5,000/min viral spike that is dozens of 180MB decodes at once, crossing the 2GB limit and triggering OOM kills that drop in-flight work.

title: Replace fixed exponential backoff with jittered backoff to break a retry thundering herd on a flaky webhook target
business_context: A B2B SaaS sends webhooks to customer endpoints via a worker. When a large customer's endpoint returned 503s for 4 minutes, 50,000 queued webhook jobs all backed off on the same exponential schedule and retried in synchronized waves, hammering the recovering endpoint with 50k simultaneous requests and re-triggering its outage three times.
constraints: ~50k webhook deliveries can be queued per customer endpoint
Worker runs 6 replicas, concurrency 50 each
Must respect per-endpoint rate limits the customer configures (e.g., 100 rps)
Retry up to 8 attempts over ~6 hours before DLQ
Delivery order is best-effort, not strict

before_state.description: All jobs failing at the same time share a deterministic exponential backoff, so retries align into synchronized bursts.

issues: Deterministic backoff aligns all retries from the same outage into one wave
No per-endpoint concurrency/rate limit
A recovering endpoint gets a 50k-request spike

reasoning: Naive fix: increase the base delay so waves are further apart — rejected because it still produces synchronized waves, just slower, and worsens latency for healthy endpoints. Alternative: a global token bucket across all endpoints — rejected because one slow endpoint would throttle delivery to everyone. Chosen path: custom jittered backoff (decorrelated jitter) plus a per-endpoint BullMQ rate limiter (group keys). Cross-layer trace: enqueue tags jobs with a group key = endpoint so the rate limiter is per-endpoint (config); a custom backoff strategy returns decorrelated-jitter delays so retries spread (async); the worker honors the customer-configured rps via the limiter (data/config); tests assert retry timestamps are spread and the limiter caps rps; rollout flags jitter per customer.

after_state.description: Retries use decorrelated jitter and per-endpoint rate limiting, so a recovering endpoint sees a smooth ramp, not a wave.

files.purpose: Decorrelated-jitter backoff spreads retries from a shared outage across the window
Per-endpoint rate limiting via groupKey caps requests to one customer regardless of replica count
Asserts retry delays are widely spread instead of identical

migration_strategy: Deploy custom backoff strategy but keep enqueue using exponential (no change) behind a JITTER flag
Enable JITTER for one internal test endpoint and confirm retries spread in metrics
Enable per-endpoint limiter with a high max, then lower to customer-configured rps
Ramp jitter to all customers over 2 days, watching recovering-endpoint error rates
Remove old exponential enqueue path
testing_strategy: Fixture enqueues 2,000 jobs to a mock endpoint that 503s then recovers; assert the request arrival histogram has no single-second spike above the configured rps (side-effect) and all jobs eventually deliver or DLQ (output). Chaos test: hold the endpoint at 503 for the full retry window and assert jobs land in DLQ after 8 attempts with spread timestamps, never a synchronized burst.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Deterministic exponential backoff means every job that failed at the same instant computes the same next-retry time, so 50k retries fire in the same second, and that synchronized herd re-overloads the endpoint exactly as it recovers.

title: Migrate a Postgres SELECT-FOR-UPDATE job table to SKIP LOCKED to eliminate worker contention at scale
business_context: A logistics platform dispatches route-optimization jobs from a Postgres jobs table. As workers scaled from 4 to 60, every worker took SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the same oldest row, serializing all polling behind one lock; throughput plateaued at 90 jobs/sec while CPU sat at 30% and dispatch latency for time-critical deliveries rose to 8 minutes.
constraints: Up to 200 concurrent workers across 3 regions
Postgres 14, single primary, ~2M rows/day through the table
A job must be picked by exactly one worker
Stuck jobs (worker died mid-process) must become reclaimable after a lease timeout
No external broker allowed (regulatory data-residency keeps it in Postgres)

before_state.description: Workers contend on the same row with FOR UPDATE and no SKIP LOCKED, so polling serializes and throughput is lock-bound.

issues: FOR UPDATE without SKIP LOCKED blocks all workers behind the lock on the oldest row
No lease/heartbeat, so a crashed worker leaves a job stuck in running forever
ORDER BY + LIMIT 1 makes contention worst-case on the hottest row

reasoning: Naive fix: add NOWAIT and retry on lock errors — rejected because it converts blocking into a busy retry storm and still funnels everyone at the same row. Alternative: move to Redis/BullMQ — rejected because data-residency rules forbid storing job payloads outside the regulated Postgres. Chosen path: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED with a batch claim and a lease column reclaimed by a sweeper. Cross-layer trace: poller claims a small batch with SKIP LOCKED so each worker grabs different rows (data), stamps a lease_until and locked_by (data); a heartbeat extends the lease while processing (async); a sweeper resets expired-lease running rows to pending (async); an index on (status, created_at) keeps the scan cheap (config); tests assert no two workers claim the same row and that crashed leases reclaim.

after_state.description: SKIP LOCKED lets every worker claim a distinct batch with no contention; leases plus a sweeper recover crashed jobs.

files.purpose: Contention-free batch claim with SKIP LOCKED plus a lease and heartbeat
Reclaims jobs from dead workers once the lease expires

files.content:
ALTER TABLE jobs ADD COLUMN locked_by text, ADD COLUMN lease_until timestamptz;
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_jobs_pending ON jobs (created_at) WHERE status='pending';
files.purpose: Adds lease columns and a partial index that keeps the SKIP LOCKED scan cheap
Asserts concurrent workers claim disjoint rows

migration_strategy: Add lease columns and partial index with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (no lock)
Deploy claimBatch behind a flag; keep old claimJob default, run claimBatch on 5% of workers
Compare throughput and confirm no duplicate claims, then flip all workers to claimBatch
Enable the sweeper on a 10s cron once lease semantics are verified
Drop the old claimJob code and FOR UPDATE path
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds 100 pending jobs and runs 20 concurrent claimBatch calls; assert the union of returned ids has no duplicates (side-effect) and total claimed equals min(100, 20*size) (output). Chaos test: claim a job, never heartbeat, advance time past the lease, run the sweeper, and assert the job returns to pending and is then re-claimable by another worker exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: FOR UPDATE without SKIP LOCKED makes every worker block on the row lock of the single oldest pending row, so 60 workers process strictly one-at-a-time; throughput is capped by lock handoff latency, not CPU, which is why scaling workers did nothing.

title: Fix lost cron jobs by replacing setInterval scheduling with BullMQ repeatable jobs across multiple replicas
business_context: A devtools SaaS runs nightly usage-aggregation via setInterval inside the API process. When the API scaled to 5 replicas, the cron fired 5 times concurrently, double-counting usage and over-billing 230 accounts; worse, during a deploy that restarted all replicas at 23:59, no replica was alive at midnight and aggregation was skipped entirely.
constraints: API runs 3-8 replicas behind a load balancer
Each scheduled task must run exactly once per interval cluster-wide
Must survive deploys/restarts without skipping or duplicating runs
Aggregation reads 40M usage rows; one run takes ~6 minutes
Timezone is fixed to UTC for billing

before_state.description: Scheduling lives in the API process with setInterval, so every replica runs it and restarts can miss the window.

issues: Runs once per replica, so N replicas = N concurrent aggregations and double billing
Drift: setInterval is not wall-clock aligned to midnight UTC
A restart near the tick skips the run entirely

reasoning: Naive fix: only run the interval if process.env.LEADER==='true' on one pinned pod — rejected because a pinned leader is a single point of failure and deploys still skip the tick. Alternative: OS-level Kubernetes CronJob — rejected because the team needs in-app retries, observability, and dependency on the existing Redis/BullMQ stack, and a one-shot pod cold-start adds 40s. Chosen path: BullMQ repeatable jobs with a cron pattern; the scheduler is idempotent across replicas because BullMQ stores the repeat in Redis. Cross-layer trace: any replica registers the repeatable job with a stable key so duplicate registrations dedupe (config); the queue produces exactly one delayed job per cron tick regardless of replica count (async); a dedicated worker (not the API) processes it with concurrency 1 (async); aggregation writes are wrapped in an idempotency guard keyed by date (data); tests assert single execution across simulated replicas.

after_state.description: BullMQ repeatable jobs schedule exactly one run per tick cluster-wide; a single-concurrency worker processes it idempotently.

files.purpose: Registers one repeatable cron job in Redis; safe to call from every replica
Processes the tick once with concurrency 1 and a per-day idempotency guard
Per-day uniqueness so a run is committed at most once even if two ticks fire
Asserts concurrent registration from 3 replicas yields one schedule

migration_strategy: Add agg_runs table; deploy scheduler registration disabled behind SCHED_BULLMQ flag
Remove the setInterval call in the same deploy so no double scheduling occurs
Enable SCHED_BULLMQ on one replica; confirm exactly one repeatable job exists in Redis
Run one nightly cycle in staging mirror to validate single execution and idempotency guard
Roll to production; monitor agg_runs has exactly one row per day
testing_strategy: Fixture calls registerSchedules from 3 simulated replicas and asserts getRepeatableJobs returns one entry (side-effect). Run the worker twice for the same day and assert aggregateUsage runs once and the second returns skipped (output + DB row count). Chaos test: simulate a restart by re-registering after a crash mid-run; assert no duplicate agg_runs row and the next tick still fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setInterval lives per-process, so each of N replicas runs the aggregation independently (double billing) and is not wall-clock aligned, so a deploy that recycles all replicas across the tick boundary leaves no live timer to fire, silently skipping a billing day.

title: Add graceful shutdown draining to a Celery worker to stop killing in-flight medical claims on deploy
business_context: A healthcare clearinghouse processes insurance claims via Celery workers. Each deploy sent SIGKILL to workers immediately, terminating ~120 in-flight claim submissions mid-call to payer APIs; some payers had already accepted the claim, so the same claim got resubmitted on restart, creating duplicate claims that triggered payer fraud flags on 3 providers.
constraints: Workers run on ECS; deploys happen 4-6 times/day
A claim submission to a payer can take up to 45s
Payers reject duplicate claim control numbers as potential fraud
ECS stop timeout is 30s before SIGKILL
Must not lose acknowledged-but-unrecorded claims

before_state.description: Workers receive SIGTERM but do not drain; tasks are killed mid-flight and unacked messages redeliver, causing duplicate submissions.

files.content:
from celery import Celery
app = Celery('claims', broker=BROKER)

@app.task(acks_late=True, bind=True)
def submit_claim(self, claim_id):
    claim = load_claim(claim_id)
    # BUG: no idempotency; resubmits on redelivery
    resp = payer_api.submit(claim)  # up to 45s
    record_ack(claim_id, resp.control_number)
# BUG: no graceful shutdown; SIGKILL kills mid-submit, message redelivers
issues: No graceful drain; SIGKILL interrupts a 45s payer call
acks_late + no idempotency means redelivery resubmits already-accepted claims
No record of in-progress control numbers to dedupe on

reasoning: Naive fix: switch to acks_early so messages ack before processing — rejected because then a crash loses the claim entirely, which for healthcare billing is unacceptable. Alternative: raise ECS stop timeout to 120s — rejected because deploys would stall and a 45s task started at T-1s still gets killed. Chosen path: graceful warm shutdown that stops prefetching and finishes in-flight tasks, combined with a control-number idempotency record so redelivery is safe. Cross-layer trace: ECS sends SIGTERM; Celery worker enters warm shutdown, stops consuming, drains in-flight tasks within the grace window (async); a submit acquires a per-claim control number and persists it before the payer call so redelivery reuses it (data); the payer dedupes on control number (data); soft/hard time limits bound task length; tests cover drain and redelivery dedupe.

after_state.description: Workers warm-shutdown to drain in-flight claims; a pre-persisted control number makes redelivery a safe no-op.

files.purpose: Pre-persisted control number makes redelivery idempotent; warm shutdown stops new consumption
Gives the worker a 60s grace window so a 45s claim can finish draining

files.content:
def reserve_control_number(claim_id):
    cn = generate_control_number()
    # unique index on claim_id guarantees one control number per claim
    db.execute(
        'INSERT INTO claim_controls(claim_id, control_number) VALUES (%s,%s) '
        'ON CONFLICT (claim_id) DO UPDATE SET claim_id=EXCLUDED.claim_id RETURNING control_number',
        (claim_id, cn))
    return db.fetchone()['control_number']
files.purpose: Atomically reserves a stable control number per claim so retries reuse it

files.content:
def test_redelivery_does_not_resubmit(mocker):
    submit = mocker.patch('worker.payer_api.submit')
    mocker.patch('worker.payer_api.is_accepted', return_value=True)
    seed_control_number('c1', 'CN-1')
    res = submit_claim.run('c1')
    assert res['dedup'] is True
    submit.assert_not_called()
files.purpose: Asserts a redelivered claim with an accepted control number is not resubmitted

migration_strategy: Add claim_controls table and deploy reserve_control_number used only on new submits
Increase ECS stopTimeout to 60s and add the warm-shutdown signal handler
Canary one service with graceful drain; trigger a deploy and confirm zero killed-mid-flight tasks
Enable idempotent resubmit-guard for all workers; monitor duplicate-control-number payer rejections drop to zero
Remove the old acks_late-without-guard path
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds a claim and a reserved control number; run submit_claim twice and assert payer_api.submit called once and record_ack written once (output + DB). Side-effect: assert claim_controls has exactly one row per claim. Chaos test: start a submit, send SIGTERM mid-call, assert the task finishes (drain) within the grace window and that a forced redelivery afterward returns dedup without a second payer call.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With acks_late and no idempotency, SIGKILL during a 45s payer call leaves the message unacked; the broker redelivers it on restart and the worker resubmits a claim the payer already accepted, generating duplicate control numbers that payers flag as fraud.

title: Introduce priority lanes in a single overloaded queue so paid-tier exports are not starved by free-tier batch jobs
business_context: A B2B analytics SaaS runs all CSV/PDF exports through one BullMQ queue. A free-tier customer scheduled 80,000 large exports overnight; the queue is FIFO, so paid enterprise customers' interactive exports waited behind 11 hours of free-tier backlog, breaching the 2-minute paid SLA and causing two enterprise churn escalations.
constraints: Single Redis cluster, one export worker pool of 12 replicas
Paid interactive exports must start within 2 minutes (p95)
Free-tier bulk jobs must still eventually complete, no starvation
Must not require customers to change their API calls
Fairness across paid customers: no single paid customer can hog all slots

before_state.description: All exports share one FIFO queue, so a free-tier flood blocks paid interactive jobs behind it.

issues: Single FIFO queue lets bulk jobs starve interactive ones
No notion of tier or priority
No per-customer fairness so one customer can flood

reasoning: Naive fix: use BullMQ job priority numbers on one queue — rejected because BullMQ priority is a heap that degrades and still allows a single paid customer to monopolize, and a huge low-priority backlog slows the priority index. Alternative: separate Redis instances per tier — rejected as operational overkill and it cannot enforce cross-tier worker fairness. Chosen path: multiple named queues (interactive, paid-bulk, free-bulk) consumed by one worker pool using weighted round-robin, plus per-customer concurrency caps. Cross-layer trace: enqueue routes by tier+interactivity to the right queue (config); a coordinator pulls from queues by weight so interactive always gets a guaranteed share (async); per-customer group limiter prevents hogging (config); workers process whichever job the coordinator hands them (async); tests assert interactive latency under load and no starvation; rollout dual-writes.

after_state.description: Tier-routed queues with weighted scheduling and per-customer caps keep interactive exports fast while bulk drains fairly.

files.purpose: Routes jobs into tier/interactivity lanes and tags a per-customer group for fairness
Weighted lane selection guarantees interactive a majority share of worker capacity
One worker pool per lane with per-customer group limiting
Asserts interactive lane keeps its guaranteed worker share

migration_strategy: Create the three lane queues; deploy enqueue dual-write: keep old queue plus route to new lanes behind ROUTING flag off
Start lane workers in shadow consuming new lanes while old worker still drains the legacy queue
Flip ROUTING on so new jobs go only to lanes; let legacy queue drain to empty
Tune weights from observed interactive p95 latency under load
Delete the legacy single queue and its worker
testing_strategy: Fixture floods free-bulk with 5,000 jobs then enqueues 50 interactive jobs; assert interactive jobs complete within the 2-minute budget (output) while free-bulk continues draining (no starvation side-effect: free queue depth strictly decreases). Chaos test: a single paid customer enqueues 10,000 paid-bulk jobs; assert the per-customer limiter caps their throughput and other paid customers still get processed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single FIFO queue orders strictly by enqueue time, so 80,000 free-tier jobs sit ahead of every later interactive job; the worker pool drains in order and paid interactive exports cannot jump the line, blowing the 2-minute SLA for 11 hours.

title: Make a Kafka consumer's offset commit transactional with the database write to stop event loss on crash
business_context: An IoT platform consumes device-telemetry events from Kafka and writes them to TimescaleDB. The consumer committed offsets on a timer independent of the DB write; when a pod crashed after an auto-commit but before the DB insert, ~9,000 telemetry points per crash vanished, leaving gaps in customer dashboards that triggered false 'device offline' alerts.
constraints: Topic has 48 partitions; 16 consumer instances
~120k events/sec sustained
At-least-once is required; gaps are unacceptable, duplicates are tolerable (Timescale upsert)
DB writes are batched (1k rows) for throughput
Auto-commit was every 5s

before_state.description: Auto-commit runs on a timer unrelated to the DB write, so a crash between commit and insert loses the batch.

issues: autoCommit advances offsets independent of the DB write
Crash between commit and insert loses the whole batch
No manual commit tied to write success

reasoning: Naive fix: shorten autoCommitInterval to 100ms — rejected because it only narrows the loss window and triples commit overhead without closing it. Alternative: Kafka transactions (exactly-once) with a transactional producer writing offsets — rejected because the sink is TimescaleDB not Kafka, so EOS doesn't span the external DB and adds heavy coordinator overhead at 120k/sec. Chosen path: disable auto-commit and manually commit offsets only after the DB batch insert succeeds, with idempotent upserts to absorb the resulting duplicates. Cross-layer trace: consumer disables autoCommit (config); eachBatch parses, upserts the batch into Timescale, and only then commits the resolved offset (async+data); upsert on (device_id, ts) makes redelivery idempotent (data); heartbeat is resolved within the batch to avoid rebalance kicks (async); tests assert no gaps after a crash.

after_state.description: Offsets commit only after the DB batch lands; idempotent upserts make at-least-once safe with no gaps.

files.purpose: Manual commit strictly after the DB upsert so a crash never advances past unwritten data
Idempotent upsert absorbs duplicates from at-least-once redelivery

files.content: ALTER TABLE telemetry ADD CONSTRAINT telemetry_pk PRIMARY KEY (device_id, ts);
files.purpose: Composite key required for the upsert dedupe to work
Asserts a crash between write and commit replays with zero gaps

migration_strategy: Add the composite primary key (online with CONCURRENTLY-style table rewrite in a maintenance window for Timescale hypertable)
Deploy consumer group telemetry-v2 with manual commit reading from telemetry's committed offsets, in shadow on 4 partitions
Verify gap metric is zero and duplicate-upsert rate is small, then cut all partitions to v2
Leave the old auto-commit group paused for rollback for 48h
Decommission the old consumer once stable
testing_strategy: Fixture produces 1,000 ordered telemetry events; run the consumer to offset 500, simulate a crash after upsert but before commit, restart, and assert the DB ends with exactly 1,000 rows (output) and no gap in the ts sequence (side-effect). Chaos test: kill the consumer mid-batch repeatedly and assert the final row count equals the produced count with duplicates collapsed by the upsert.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Timer-based auto-commit advances the committed offset every 5s regardless of whether the DB insert ran; a crash in the window after a commit but before insertBatch permanently skips those offsets on restart, dropping ~9,000 telemetry points and tearing gaps in dashboards.

title: Build a saga with compensations to replace a brittle multi-step async checkout job in a gaming marketplace
business_context: A gaming marketplace's purchase flow runs as one big async job: charge card, grant in-game item, decrement inventory, notify seller. When item-grant failed after a successful charge, the job just errored and retried, re-charging buyers; ~600 players were double-charged in a flash sale and the team had to issue manual refunds for 3 days.
constraints: Steps span 3 services: payments (Stripe), inventory (Postgres), game-grant (gRPC)
Each step can fail independently; partial completion must be reconciled
No distributed XA transactions available across these systems
Buyers must never be charged without receiving the item, and never double-charged
Throughput: 4,000 purchases/min at peak flash sales

before_state.description: A single job performs all steps with no compensation; a mid-flow failure retries from the top, repeating already-completed side effects.

issues: A failure after charge retries the whole job and re-charges the buyer
No compensation to refund a charge when grant fails
No per-step state so retries can't resume mid-flow

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap everything in try/catch and refund on failure inline — rejected because a crash between charge and the catch loses the refund intent entirely. Alternative: a heavyweight orchestrator framework (Temporal) — rejected for this scope as it requires a new runtime and migration the team can't take on this quarter; they already run BullMQ. Chosen path: a persisted saga where each step is a separately-retryable child job with recorded state and explicit compensations executed by a reverse path. Cross-layer trace: API creates a saga row with steps (data); a flow producer enqueues step jobs as a BullMQ flow so children run with their own retries (async); each step is idempotent and records completion (data); on a step's terminal failure, a compensation handler runs recorded compensations in reverse (refund charge, restock) (async); tests assert no double-charge and full compensation; rollout shadow-runs.

after_state.description: A persisted saga runs idempotent steps with recorded state and reverse compensations, so failure refunds rather than re-charges.

files.purpose: Generic saga runner with persisted per-step state and reverse-order compensation

files.content:
export const purchaseSteps: Step[] = [
  { name: 'charge',
    run: async (c) => { c.chargeId = (await stripe.charge(c.buyer, c.price, { idempotencyKey: c.sagaId })).id; },
    compensate: async (c) => { if (c.chargeId) await stripe.refund(c.chargeId, { idempotencyKey: 'r-' + c.sagaId }); } },
  { name: 'grant',
    run: async (c) => { await game.grantItem(c.buyer, c.itemId, c.sagaId); },
    compensate: async (c) => { await game.revokeItem(c.buyer, c.itemId, c.sagaId); } },
  { name: 'inventory',
    run: async (c) => { await inventory.decrement(c.itemId, c.sagaId); },
    compensate: async (c) => { await inventory.increment(c.itemId, c.sagaId); } },
  { name: 'notify', run: async (c) => { await notifySeller(c.itemId); }, compensate: async () => {} },
];
files.purpose: Defines each purchase step with an idempotent action and its compensation
Persists per-step status so retries resume and compensations are tracked
Asserts a failed grant triggers exactly one charge and a compensating refund

migration_strategy: Add saga_steps table; deploy saga runner used only for new purchases behind SAGA flag, old worker unchanged
Enable SAGA for internal test accounts; verify compensation paths in staging with injected failures
Ramp to 5% of live purchases during a non-sale window, watch refund-on-failure metric and double-charge metric (should be zero)
Ramp to 100% before the next flash sale
Remove the legacy monolithic purchase worker
testing_strategy: Fixture runs a full purchase and asserts charge, grant, inventory decrement, and notify all happen once (output + DB step rows all 'done'). Side-effect: assert inventory decremented exactly once. Chaos test: force grant to fail; assert charge is compensated by a refund with an idempotency key, inventory is not decremented, and the buyer is not double-charged even after BullMQ retries the saga.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With one job and attempts:5, any failure after the Stripe charge throws and BullMQ re-runs the whole handler from the top, re-charging the buyer each retry because the charge step has no idempotency key and no compensation, producing the double-charges seen in the flash sale.

title: Add per-tenant queue isolation to stop one noisy tenant's jobs from starving all others in a shared worker pool
business_context: A multi-tenant B2B document-processing SaaS runs all tenants' OCR jobs in one BullMQ queue. A single tenant uploaded a 200,000-page archive; their jobs flooded the queue and every other tenant's documents waited 6+ hours, breaching contractual 15-minute SLAs and triggering credits for 40 enterprise accounts.
constraints: ~1,200 active tenants; jobs are CPU-heavy OCR (avg 4s each)
Worker pool is 30 replicas, concurrency 4
No tenant may consume more than a fair share when others have work
Idle capacity should still be usable by a single tenant (work-conserving)
Cannot create 1,200 physical queues (Redis key explosion)

before_state.description: All tenants share one FIFO queue, so a single tenant's flood blocks everyone behind it.

issues: One tenant's 200k jobs block all others (head-of-line)
No fairness across tenants
No concurrency cap per tenant

reasoning: Naive fix: one BullMQ queue per tenant — rejected because 1,200 queues each needing a worker connection causes Redis key and connection explosion and uneven idle capacity. Alternative: hard per-tenant rate caps — rejected because they are not work-conserving; a lone tenant on an idle system would be throttled for no reason. Chosen path: BullMQ groups (one logical queue, per-tenant group keys) with round-robin fairness across groups plus a soft per-tenant concurrency cap. Cross-layer trace: enqueue tags each job with group id = tenantId (config); the worker uses BullMQ Pro-style group round-robin so it dequeues across tenants fairly (async); a per-group concurrency cap bounds a single tenant when others are active but relaxes when idle (config); metrics expose per-tenant wait time (observability); tests assert fairness and work-conservation.

after_state.description: A single queue with per-tenant groups gives round-robin fairness and bounded per-tenant concurrency while staying work-conserving.

files.purpose: Tags jobs with a per-tenant group so the scheduler can round-robin across tenants
Round-robin group scheduling plus a soft per-tenant concurrency cap
Surfaces per-tenant wait time and SLA breaches for alerting
Asserts a small tenant is not starved behind a flooding tenant

migration_strategy: Deploy group-aware enqueue dual-writing group id while still using a plain worker (no behavior change)
Switch one canary worker replica to group scheduling and compare per-tenant wait histograms
Roll group scheduling to all replicas once fairness is confirmed
Tune per-tenant group concurrency cap from observed SLA-breach metrics
Remove the non-grouped enqueue path
testing_strategy: Fixture enqueues 5,000 jobs for tenant big and 10 for tenant small; assert small's max wait < 60s (output) while big still makes progress (side-effect: big's completed count rises steadily). Chaos test: with only tenant big present and the system otherwise idle, assert big bursts above the per-tenant cap (work-conserving), proving the cap only binds under contention.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single FIFO queue serializes by enqueue time, so tenant big's 200,000 jobs sit ahead of every later job; with no per-tenant fairness the worker pool drains them in order and every other tenant waits hours, breaching the 15-minute SLA.

title: Bound an unbounded fan-out so a single parent job stops exploding Redis memory and crashing the queue
business_context: A marketing SaaS sends campaign emails by fanning a parent 'campaign' job into one child job per recipient. A 9-million-recipient campaign enqueued 9M child jobs in seconds; Redis memory jumped 14GB, hit maxmemory, started evicting keys from OTHER queues, and the whole job system stalled across the company.
constraints: Redis is shared by 25 services; maxmemory-policy is noeviction for job data
Campaigns range from 100 to 12M recipients
Child jobs must still retry individually on send failure
Must not evict or corrupt other services' queues
Send rate is capped at 50k emails/min by the provider

before_state.description: The parent enqueues every recipient as a child job at once, so Redis memory scales linearly with recipient count and exhausts shared memory.

issues: Enqueues up to 12M jobs synchronously, exhausting shared Redis
Loads all recipients into memory at once
No pacing toward the 50k/min provider cap

reasoning: Naive fix: enqueue children in chunks of 1,000 — rejected because even chunked, all 12M still land in Redis quickly and exhaust memory; chunking the producer doesn't bound the resident set. Alternative: store recipients in Redis as a list and pop them — rejected because the list itself is the 12M-element memory blob. Chosen path: a cursor-based drip producer that keeps only a small window of child jobs resident, paced to the provider cap, with recipients streamed from Postgres. Cross-layer trace: parent stores a cursor and self-reschedules to enqueue the next window only when in-flight depth is below a watermark (async); recipients are streamed via a keyset paginated DB query (data); a rate limiter paces sends to 50k/min (config); child jobs retry individually (async); tests assert bounded queue depth on a huge campaign.

after_state.description: A cursor-driven drip keeps resident child jobs bounded and paced; recipients stream from Postgres so memory stays flat regardless of campaign size.

files.purpose: Drips children in bounded windows with backpressure and a DB keyset cursor
Streams recipients with keyset pagination so memory is independent of campaign size
Sends children with retries and paces to the 50k/min provider cap
Asserts resident child-job count stays bounded for a massive campaign

migration_strategy: Add keyset index on campaign_recipients(campaign_id, id); deploy drip producer behind FANOUT_DRIP flag off
Enable drip for campaigns under 50k recipients first; verify identical sends and lower Redis memory
Enable for all sizes; monitor sendQueue waiting depth stays under the window
Add an alert on Redis memory and on fanout backlog age
Remove the all-at-once enqueue path
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds 12M recipients and runs the fanout to completion while polling sendQueue depth; assert peak waiting depth stays under ~40k (side-effect) and every recipient gets exactly one send job (output, count match). Chaos test: pause the send worker so children accumulate; assert the producer backs off (reschedules with delay) instead of enqueueing more, keeping depth at the watermark.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Enqueuing one child per recipient synchronously means a 12M campaign writes 12M job hashes into shared Redis in seconds, blowing past maxmemory; with noeviction the writes fail and with eviction other services' queue keys get evicted, stalling job processing company-wide.

title: Add a circuit breaker around a flaky downstream so a worker stops retry-storming a dependency that's already down
business_context: A fintech reconciliation worker calls a third-party banking API. When that API had a 20-minute outage, 200k queued jobs each retried 5 times against the dead endpoint, sending ~1M failed requests that the bank's WAF interpreted as an attack and rate-limited the company's entire IP range for 2 hours, blocking even healthy traffic.
constraints: Banking API has an undocumented 600 rps soft limit; bursts get IP-banned
Worker pool: 10 replicas, concurrency 30 (300 in-flight)
Must not lose jobs during a downstream outage
When the API recovers, work must resume automatically
Circuit state must be shared across all replicas

before_state.description: Every job independently retries against the downstream with no shared awareness that it is down, producing a self-inflicted retry storm.

issues: No circuit breaker; all jobs keep calling a down API
Fixed backoff produces synchronized retry waves
Retries across 200k jobs generate ~1M requests during one outage

reasoning: Naive fix: a per-process in-memory circuit breaker (opossum) — rejected because each of 10 replicas keeps its own state, so 10 breakers still let 10x bursts through and don't coordinate recovery. Alternative: just lower attempts to 1 — rejected because genuine transient failures then permanently fail and reconciliation gaps require manual recovery. Chosen path: a Redis-backed shared circuit breaker so all replicas open together, with worker pausing while open and a half-open probe. Cross-layer trace: the worker checks shared breaker state in Redis before calling (async); failures increment a sliding window; when the failure threshold trips, the breaker opens and the worker pauses the queue cluster-wide (async+config); a single probe job tests recovery (half-open) before closing; jobs are not consumed while open so nothing is lost (data); tests assert no calls flow while open and auto-resume.

after_state.description: A Redis-backed shared circuit breaker opens for all replicas at once, pauses consumption while the downstream is down, and half-open probes recovery.

files.purpose: Shared cross-replica breaker state in Redis with sliding-window failure counting
Checks the shared breaker before each call and trips it on downstream failures
Asserts the open breaker stops nearly all downstream calls

migration_strategy: Deploy RedisCircuit in observe-only mode logging would-open events without pausing
Tune failures/window thresholds from the observed outage pattern
Enable breaker enforcement on one canary replica; trigger a simulated downstream 503 and confirm cluster-wide open via shared state
Roll enforcement to all replicas; verify request volume to the bank drops to near-zero during outages
Remove observe-only mode
testing_strategy: Fixture makes the downstream fail; run 200 jobs and assert the breaker reaches open and that subsequent jobs make fewer than 5 downstream calls (side-effect on call count). Assert jobs are rescheduled, not lost (output: jobs remain in queue). Chaos test: recover the downstream and assert the half-open probe closes the breaker and processing resumes automatically without manual intervention.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-job retries with no shared circuit state mean every one of 200k jobs across 10 replicas keeps calling the dead API; 5 retries each yields ~1M requests in 20 minutes, tripping the bank's WAF and getting the whole IP range banned.

title: Fix at-most-once SQS processing that silently drops messages by moving ack to after successful handling
business_context: An e-commerce order-fulfillment service consumes SQS messages and creates warehouse pick tickets. The consumer deleted each message from SQS immediately on receive, then processed it; when handling threw (bad SKU mapping), the message was already gone, so ~3,400 orders never got pick tickets and shipped late, costing a peak-season SLA penalty.
constraints: SQS standard queue; visibility timeout currently 30s
Handling can take up to 25s (calls a WMS gRPC service)
At-least-once required; lost orders are unacceptable
Must handle SQS at-least-once duplicates idempotently
10 consumer instances, long-polling

before_state.description: Messages are deleted before processing, so any handler failure permanently loses the order.

issues: Deletes the message before processing succeeds (at-most-once)
No idempotency for SQS duplicate deliveries
Visibility timeout (30s) shorter than max handling (25s) is risky under GC pauses

reasoning: Naive fix: just move the delete after processing — necessary but insufficient because SQS may redeliver (at-least-once) and createPickTicket isn't idempotent, so the same order would get two pick tickets. Alternative: switch to a FIFO queue for exactly-once — rejected because FIFO caps throughput at 300 msg/s with batching and the order volume exceeds that at peak. Chosen path: delete only after success, add an idempotency key on pick-ticket creation, and extend visibility while processing. Cross-layer trace: consumer processes first, deletes after (async); createPickTicket upserts on orderId so duplicates are no-ops (data); a visibility-timeout heartbeat extends the lock for long WMS calls (async); a redrive policy routes repeated failures to a DLQ (config); tests assert no loss on failure and no duplicate ticket on redelivery.

after_state.description: Messages are deleted only after successful, idempotent processing; visibility is extended for long handlers and a DLQ catches poison messages.

files.purpose: Acks only after successful processing and extends visibility during long handling
Idempotent pick-ticket creation keyed on order_id absorbs duplicate deliveries
Heartbeats the visibility timeout so a 25s WMS call isn't redelivered mid-flight
Asserts a failed handler leaves the message in the queue for redelivery

migration_strategy: Add pick_tickets unique index on order_id; deploy idempotent createPickTicket (safe with old consumer too)
Configure SQS redrive policy to a DLQ with maxReceiveCount 5 and raise visibility timeout to 60s
Deploy the ack-after-success consumer to one canary instance; verify no duplicate tickets and no losses
Roll to all consumers; monitor DLQ for genuine poison messages
Remove the old delete-first code
testing_strategy: Fixture delivers an order message; on success assert exactly one pick_ticket row and one WMS ensurePick call (output + side-effects), and the SQS message is deleted. Deliver the same message twice and assert still one pick ticket (idempotency). Chaos test: make the handler throw; assert deleteMessage is NOT called so the message redelivers, and after a code fix the redelivery creates the ticket exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Deleting the SQS message before processing means the only durable copy is gone the instant handling begins; a handler exception (bad SKU mapping) leaves no message to retry, so the order silently vanishes and never produces a pick ticket.

title: Eliminate a poison-pill JSON parse that crashes a Go Kafka worker and restart-loops the whole consumer group
business_context: A logistics tracking service runs a Go Kafka consumer that unmarshals shipment events. A misbehaving upstream produced a malformed event; json.Unmarshal returned an error the code ignored, then a nil-pointer deref on the partial struct panicked the process. Kubernetes restarted it, it re-read the same offset, panicked again — a crash loop that took shipment tracking offline for 50 minutes.
constraints: Go service, segmentio/kafka-go, 12 partitions, 6 pods
Must not lose valid events; poison events must be quarantined
Panic in one goroutine must not take down the pod
Consumer must commit past poison messages to avoid crash loops
Quarantined events need enough context to reprocess after upstream fix

before_state.description: Unmarshal errors are ignored and a nil deref panics the whole process, which then crash-loops on the same offset.

issues: Unmarshal error ignored, leaving a zero/partial struct
Nil-pointer deref panics the whole process
No DLQ; the bad offset is re-read forever, crash-looping

reasoning: Naive fix: add `if err != nil { continue }` and rely on auto-commit — rejected because continuing without committing means the offset isn't advanced and the same poison message is re-read, and a panic elsewhere still kills the pod. Alternative: validate with a JSON schema before unmarshal — rejected as insufficient alone because it doesn't recover offsets or quarantine. Chosen path: per-message recover() guard, explicit error handling, DLQ topic for poison messages, and manual commit after handling (success or quarantine). Cross-layer trace: each message is processed in a function with a deferred recover so a panic is contained (async); unmarshal/validation errors route the raw bytes to a dlq topic with metadata (data); the consumer commits the offset after success OR quarantine so it never re-reads poison (async); a reprocess tool reads the DLQ after the upstream fix (data); tests cover panic containment and offset advance.

after_state.description: Each message is handled under a recover guard; malformed events are quarantined to a DLQ and the offset commits past them, so no crash loop occurs.

files.purpose: Contains panics with recover, validates input, and quarantines poison messages while committing past them
Writes poison messages to a DLQ topic with full reprocess context
Replays quarantined events back to the main topic after the upstream is fixed
Asserts a poison message is quarantined and the offset advances without panicking the process

migration_strategy: Create shipments.dlq topic; deploy the recover-guarded handler with DLQ writes behind a feature build flag in staging
Replay a captured malformed event in staging and confirm no crash loop and a DLQ entry
Deploy to one production pod (canary) and observe panic-free processing
Roll to all 6 pods; alert on DLQ growth
After the upstream producer is fixed, run Reprocess for the quarantined offsets
testing_strategy: Fixture feeds a mix of valid and malformed events; assert valid events call process (output) and malformed ones land in the DLQ with orig_offset header (side-effect), and the consumer commits past them. Chaos test: feed an event that triggers a nil-deref panic and assert the pod stays alive, the offset advances, and the event is quarantined rather than re-read in a loop.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Ignoring the Unmarshal error leaves evt.Shipment nil; the deref panics the whole process; because the offset was never committed, the restarted pod re-reads the identical poison message and panics again, an infinite crash loop that takes the consumer group offline.

title: Replace at-once Sidekiq retry of a non-idempotent payout job with a state machine to stop double payouts
business_context: A creator-payments platform pays out earnings via a Sidekiq job that calls Stripe Transfers. When the job timed out after Stripe created the transfer, Sidekiq retried and created a SECOND transfer; 280 creators were overpaid in one cycle, costing $94k that took weeks to claw back.
constraints: Ruby on Rails + Sidekiq; 25 concurrent workers
Payouts can be $1 to $50k; double-pays are catastrophic
Stripe transfer calls can take up to 20s and may succeed after a client timeout
Must support partial-failure resume across the payout batch
Sidekiq default retry is 25 attempts

before_state.description: The job calls Stripe transfer directly with no state tracking, so a timeout-then-retry creates a duplicate transfer.

files.content:
class PayoutJob
  include Sidekiq::Job
  def perform(payout_id)
    payout = Payout.find(payout_id)
    # BUG: no idempotency key; retry after timeout creates a 2nd transfer
    transfer = Stripe::Transfer.create(amount: payout.cents, destination: payout.stripe_account)
    payout.update!(status: 'paid', transfer_id: transfer.id)
  end
end
issues: No Stripe idempotency key, so a retry duplicates the transfer
Status is only written after the call; a crash mid-call loses the transfer_id
No state machine to know whether the transfer was already initiated

reasoning: Naive fix: set sidekiq_options retry: 0 — rejected because a transient Stripe 500 then permanently fails the payout and a human must reconcile. Alternative: a DB advisory lock around the perform — rejected because it serializes payouts and still doesn't prevent a duplicate if the process dies after the Stripe call but before the status write. Chosen path: a payout state machine (pending -> initiating -> paid/failed) plus a Stripe idempotency key derived from the payout id so retries reuse the same transfer. Cross-layer trace: job transitions payout to initiating and persists an idempotency_key before calling Stripe (data); Stripe::Transfer.create passes that key so a retry returns the SAME transfer rather than creating a new one (data); on success it transitions to paid recording transfer_id (data); a reconciler queries Stripe for initiating payouts older than N minutes to settle ambiguity (async); tests assert single transfer across retries.

after_state.description: A persisted state machine plus a Stripe idempotency key make payout creation exactly-once across retries and crashes.

files.content:
class PayoutJob
  include Sidekiq::Job
  sidekiq_options retry: 8, queue: :payouts
  def perform(payout_id)
    payout = Payout.find(payout_id)
    return if payout.paid?
    payout.update!(status: 'initiating', idempotency_key: payout.idempotency_key || SecureRandom.uuid) if payout.pending?
    transfer = Stripe::Transfer.create(
      { amount: payout.cents, destination: payout.stripe_account, currency: 'usd' },
      { idempotency_key: payout.idempotency_key } # retry reuses the same transfer
    )
    payout.update!(status: 'paid', transfer_id: transfer.id)
  end
end
files.purpose: State machine plus Stripe idempotency key makes transfer creation exactly-once

files.content:
class PayoutState < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.1]
  def change
    add_column :payouts, :idempotency_key, :string
    add_column :payouts, :status, :string, default: 'pending', null: false
    add_index :payouts, :idempotency_key, unique: true
  end
end
files.purpose: Persists the idempotency key and status so retries and reconciliation can resume

files.content:
class PayoutReconciler
  include Sidekiq::Job
  def perform
    Payout.where(status: 'initiating').where('updated_at < ?', 5.minutes.ago).find_each do |p|
      # ask Stripe whether the keyed transfer exists before re-attempting
      existing = Stripe::Transfer.list(transfer_group: p.idempotency_key).data.first
      if existing
        p.update!(status: 'paid', transfer_id: existing.id)
      else
        p.update!(status: 'pending') # safe to retry the create
      end
    end
  end
end
files.purpose: Resolves stuck initiating payouts by querying Stripe rather than blindly retrying

files.content:
it 'creates exactly one transfer across retries' do
  payout = create(:payout, status: 'pending')
  expect(Stripe::Transfer).to receive(:create).once.and_return(double(id: 'tr_1'))
  PayoutJob.new.perform(payout.id)
  PayoutJob.new.perform(payout.id) # simulated retry
  expect(payout.reload.status).to eq('paid')
end
files.purpose: Asserts a retried payout job creates only one Stripe transfer

migration_strategy: Run migration to add status and idempotency_key (default pending) backfilling existing paid rows to status='paid'
Deploy the keyed job behind a PAYOUT_IDEMPOTENT flag, defaulting to the old path
Enable for a small payout batch and verify Stripe shows one transfer per payout even when retried
Enable for all payouts; schedule the reconciler every 5 minutes
Remove the legacy non-keyed code path
testing_strategy: Fixture creates a pending payout and runs the job twice; assert Stripe::Transfer.create is called once and the payout ends 'paid' with a transfer_id (output + DB). Side-effect: assert only one idempotency_key row. Chaos test: simulate a crash after the Stripe call but before the status write (leave status 'initiating'); run the reconciler and assert it settles to 'paid' from Stripe without creating a second transfer.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without an idempotency key, a client timeout after Stripe created the transfer makes Sidekiq retry and call Stripe::Transfer.create again, creating a second real money movement; with 25 retries the same payout could be sent many times.

title: Convert a synchronous report-generation endpoint into an async job with status polling to fix request timeouts
business_context: A B2B SaaS generates 90-day analytics PDFs inline in an HTTP request. As datasets grew, generation took 40-90s, exceeding the 30s ALB idle timeout; requests returned 504s, clients retried, and each retry spawned another 90s CPU-heavy generation, compounding load until the API tier fell over twice in a week.
constraints: ALB idle timeout is 30s and cannot be raised above 60s by policy
Report generation is CPU-bound, 40-90s, must not run in the request thread
Clients are browser SPAs and a public API; both need a stable contract
A given (user, report-params) should not generate twice concurrently
Results must be downloadable for 24h

before_state.description: The report is generated synchronously inside the request handler, blocking the connection past the load-balancer timeout.

issues: Long CPU work blocks the request past the 30s ALB timeout
504s cause client retries that spawn duplicate generations
No way to dedupe concurrent identical report requests

reasoning: Naive fix: bump server/ALB timeouts — rejected because policy caps ALB at 60s and 90s reports still fail, and holding a connection for 90s ties up a worker thread. Alternative: stream the PDF as it generates — rejected because PDF generation here isn't incrementally streamable and the SPA needs a downloadable artifact. Chosen path: accept the request, enqueue a job, return 202 with a job id and poll/SSE for status, store the artifact in S3. Cross-layer trace: POST /reports computes a deterministic params hash, dedupes concurrent identical requests, enqueues a BullMQ job, returns 202 + jobId (UI/API); the worker generates the PDF and uploads to S3, writing status to the DB (async+data); GET /reports/:id returns status or a signed URL (API); the SPA polls or subscribes via SSE (UI); tests assert 202 contract and dedupe; rollout keeps the sync path behind a flag for old clients.

after_state.description: The endpoint enqueues an idempotent job and returns 202; a worker generates the PDF to S3 and clients poll for a signed download URL.

files.purpose: Returns 202 with a status URL and dedupes identical concurrent report requests
Generates the PDF off the request thread, stores it in S3, and tracks status

files.content:
CREATE TABLE reports (
  job_id  text PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id text NOT NULL,
  status  text NOT NULL,
  s3_key  text,
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
files.purpose: Tracks report job status and the S3 artifact key for polling
Asserts the 202 async contract and that concurrent identical requests dedupe to one job

migration_strategy: Deploy the reports table, worker, and new GET endpoint while keeping POST synchronous behind ASYNC_REPORTS=false
Update the SPA to handle both 200 (sync) and 202 (async) responses
Flip ASYNC_REPORTS=true for the SPA traffic, monitor 504 rate drop to zero
Migrate public API clients with a versioned header; deprecate sync after notice
Remove the synchronous generation path
testing_strategy: Fixture posts a report request and asserts a 202 with jobId (output); run the worker and assert the DB status goes running->done and an S3 object exists (side-effects), then GET returns a signed URL. Chaos test: post two identical requests concurrently and assert only one job runs (dedupe) and a 504-style client retry of the same params returns the same in-progress job rather than starting a second generation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Generating a 40-90s PDF inside the HTTP handler holds the connection past the 30s ALB idle timeout, so the client gets a 504 even though work is still running; the client retries, each retry launches another 90s CPU-bound generation, and the duplicated load saturates the API tier.

title: Fix a stuck-job leak where crashed workers leave BullMQ jobs in active forever, slowly draining capacity
business_context: A video-transcoding platform's BullMQ workers occasionally OOM mid-transcode. Because the workers used manual job locking incorrectly, crashed jobs stayed in the active set and were never moved to failed or retried; over 3 weeks 4,100 jobs leaked into active, the effective concurrency shrank, and transcoding throughput silently dropped 35% before anyone noticed stuck customer videos.
constraints: Transcodes take 2-30 minutes; lock duration must exceed max processing time
Workers can OOM/crash without graceful shutdown
A stalled job must be retried, not lost or duplicated mid-output
Must distinguish a slow-but-alive job from a dead one
BullMQ stalled-job detection must not falsely reclaim long-running transcodes

before_state.description: Lock duration is shorter than transcode time and stalled-check is misconfigured, so long jobs get falsely reclaimed while crashed jobs leak into active.

issues: lockDuration (30s) shorter than transcode time -> false stall reclaim or lock loss
maxStalledCount 0 means truly stalled jobs are never recovered
No progress/lock renewal heartbeat for long jobs

reasoning: Naive fix: set lockDuration to 30 minutes — rejected because then a genuinely crashed worker's job is locked for 30 minutes before any recovery, badly delaying the customer's video. Alternative: split transcode into many short chunked jobs — valuable but a large refactor; rejected for this fix as out of scope and it changes the output assembly. Chosen path: a moderate lockDuration with periodic job.updateProgress to renew the lock, correct stalled detection, and maxStalledCount>0 so dead jobs retry. Cross-layer trace: worker calls job.updateProgress on each transcode callback to renew the lock (async); lockDuration set above the heartbeat interval so an alive job never stalls (config); stalledInterval and maxStalledCount tuned so a dead worker's job is reclaimed after a bounded window (config); the transcode writes to a temp key and atomically renames on completion so a reclaimed retry doesn't serve a half file (data); tests assert renewal and reclaim.

after_state.description: Long jobs renew their lock via progress heartbeats; correct stalled settings reclaim only genuinely dead jobs and retry them, with atomic output to avoid half-files.

files.purpose: Heartbeat lock renewal keeps live jobs from stalling; correct stalled settings reclaim dead ones; atomic output prevents half-files
Wires ffmpeg progress to the lock-renewing heartbeat callback
Observability so stalled/leaked jobs are alerted instead of silently draining capacity
Asserts a long job heartbeats its lock and is not reclaimed mid-flight

migration_strategy: Deploy progress-heartbeat worker with lockDuration 60s and maxStalledCount 2 to one canary replica
Verify long transcodes complete without false stalls and that a killed worker's job is reclaimed and retried
Roll to all replicas; add alerts on stalled and stalled_exhausted counters
Add a one-time sweep that moves the 4,100 leaked active jobs back to waiting via queue.clean/retryJobs
Remove the old maxStalledCount:0 config
testing_strategy: Fixture runs a simulated 2-minute transcode emitting progress and asserts updateProgress is called periodically (side-effect) and the output is published atomically to the final key (output). Chaos test: kill the worker mid-transcode (no graceful shutdown); assert BullMQ marks the job stalled and retries it (up to maxStalledCount) and that the retry produces a complete file because the temp-then-rename publish prevents serving a partial output.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With lockDuration 30s shorter than a 30-minute transcode and maxStalledCount 0, live long jobs lose their lock or are falsely stalled, while genuinely crashed jobs are never reclaimed (count 0), so they accumulate in the active set, permanently consuming concurrency slots and silently shrinking throughput.

title: Add ordered per-key processing to a Kafka consumer so out-of-order inventory deltas stop overselling stock
business_context: An e-commerce platform applies inventory deltas (reserve/release) from a Kafka topic. The consumer processed messages concurrently across a thread pool, so a release(+5) sometimes applied before its reserve(-5), driving stock negative and overselling 1,900 units of a limited-edition sneaker during a drop, forcing cancellations and chargebacks.
constraints: ~80k inventory events/sec across 36 partitions
Events for the same SKU must apply in produced order
Throughput must stay high; cannot serialize all SKUs globally
Producer already keys messages by SKU
Consumer is Java/Spring Kafka with a concurrent listener

before_state.description: A concurrent listener dispatches messages to a shared thread pool, breaking per-SKU ordering even though messages are keyed by SKU.

issues: executor.submit breaks per-partition ordering guarantees
Same-SKU reserve/release can apply out of order
Negative stock from misordered deltas oversells

reasoning: Naive fix: set concurrency to 1 — rejected because a single thread can't keep up with 80k/sec and would build unbounded lag. Alternative: a global lock per SKU using a distributed lock service — rejected as far too much coordination overhead at 80k/sec. Chosen path: rely on Kafka's per-partition ordering by NOT offloading to an async pool, using container concurrency equal to partitions and applying deltas synchronously per partition, plus a DB-level conditional update that refuses to go negative as a safety net. Cross-layer trace: the listener processes records on the partition's own thread in offset order (async); applyDelta runs synchronously so same-SKU events (same partition) stay ordered (data); a conditional UPDATE ... WHERE qty + delta >= 0 prevents negative stock even if a bug slips through (data); concurrency matches partitions for parallelism across SKUs (config); tests assert ordering and no oversell.

after_state.description: The listener processes each partition's records synchronously in offset order, preserving per-SKU ordering, with a non-negative DB guard as a backstop.

files.purpose: Synchronous per-partition processing preserves per-SKU ordering; concurrency matches partition count for parallelism
Atomic conditional update is a backstop that cannot drive stock negative
Manual ack and per-partition concurrency so ordering and offset commits are correct
Asserts same-SKU events apply in produced order and never oversell

migration_strategy: Deploy the synchronous listener with concurrency=36 and manual ack to a canary consumer group on a copy topic
Replay a captured reserve/release sequence and confirm in-order application and zero negative stock
Add the conditional non-negative UPDATE guard as a backstop (safe with old consumer too)
Cut the production consumer group to the new listener; keep old paused for rollback
Monitor rejected_oversell metric; investigate any nonzero as an ordering bug
testing_strategy: Fixture produces an ordered reserve(-5) then release(+5) for the same SKU and asserts final qty equals the start (output) with no intermediate negative and zero oversell-rejections (side-effect on metric). Chaos test: produce a flood of interleaved deltas across many SKUs at high rate; assert each SKU's final quantity matches the deterministic sum and the conditional guard never had to reject (proving ordering held under load).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Offloading each record to a shared executor discards Kafka's per-partition ordering: two same-SKU events that arrived in order get scheduled onto different threads and can run in either order, so a release can apply before its reserve and the running quantity goes negative, overselling limited stock.

title: Decompose a monolithic ETL job into a dependency-aware job graph to enable partial retries and parallelism
business_context: A data platform runs nightly ETL as one giant Python job: extract 12 sources, transform, load to the warehouse. If any single source or transform failed at hour 5, the whole job aborted and re-ran from scratch the next night, so a flaky source repeatedly delayed all downstream dashboards by 24 hours; finance closed late 3 months running.
constraints: 12 source extracts, 30 transforms, 8 loads with real dependencies
Independent branches should run in parallel to fit a 4-hour window
A failed node must retry without re-running already-successful upstream nodes
Reruns must be reproducible (same input partition -> same output)
Must run on the existing BullMQ/Redis infra, not adopt Airflow this quarter

before_state.description: A single sequential script runs all steps; any failure aborts everything and forces a full rerun.

issues: Sequential: independent extracts can't run in parallel
No node-level retry; a single failure re-runs the entire pipeline
No memoization of successful nodes between reruns

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap each step in try/except and skip failures — rejected because skipping a failed transform produces silently incomplete warehouse data feeding finance. Alternative: adopt Airflow/Dagster — rejected for this quarter as it requires new infra, on-call training, and a migration the team can't fund now; they already run BullMQ. Chosen path: model the pipeline as a DAG of BullMQ flow jobs (parent depends on children) with per-node idempotent outputs keyed by run-date partition, so failed nodes retry independently and successful nodes are memoized. Cross-layer trace: a DAG definition declares node dependencies (config); a flow producer creates a BullMQ FlowProducer tree so a node runs only after its parents complete (async); each node checks a node_runs ledger and skips if its partition output already exists (data); independent branches run in parallel via the flow (async); a rerun reuses completed node outputs; tests assert partial retry and parallelism.

after_state.description: The pipeline is a BullMQ flow DAG; nodes are idempotent per run-date, independent branches run in parallel, and only failed nodes retry on rerun.

files.content:
# nodes declare their upstream dependencies
DAG = {
  'extract_sales': [], 'extract_users': [], 'extract_ads': [],
  'transform_revenue': ['extract_sales'],
  'transform_funnel': ['extract_users', 'extract_ads'],
  'load_finance': ['transform_revenue'],
  'load_growth': ['transform_funnel'],
}
files.purpose: Declarative dependency graph driving parallelism and ordering
Builds a BullMQ flow so each node runs only after its dependency children complete
Runs one node idempotently per run-date so successful nodes are memoized across reruns
Ledger of completed nodes per run-date enabling partial reruns
Asserts a rerun skips completed nodes and only re-executes the failed branch

migration_strategy: Add node_runs ledger; port each step into NODE_FUNCS with deterministic run_date partitioning
Run the DAG flow in parallel-shadow alongside the monolith, comparing outputs for a week
Cut the nightly schedule to the DAG; keep the monolith available as a manual fallback
Verify partial reruns work by injecting a single-node failure and confirming only it reruns
Decommission the monolithic run.py
testing_strategy: Fixture runs the full DAG for a run-date and asserts all node_runs rows exist and warehouse outputs match the monolith's golden output (output + side-effect). Assert independent branches run concurrently (timing/overlap assertion). Chaos test: make transform_revenue fail once; rerun and assert the extract nodes are skipped via the ledger and only the failed node and its descendants re-execute, producing identical final output.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single sequential monolith has no node boundaries, so a failure at hour 5 has no memoized upstream state to resume from; the only recovery is restarting all 12 extracts and 30 transforms, which can't finish before the next dashboard deadline, delaying finance by a full day.

title: Add a rate-limited token-bucket worker to respect a third-party SMS API quota and stop 429 cascades
business_context: A healthcare appointment-reminder service sends SMS via Twilio from a worker. During a morning batch, 18 replicas blasted Twilio with no global rate control; Twilio returned 429s, the worker retried immediately, and the burst tripped Twilio's account-level throttle, delaying ALL transactional SMS (including 2FA) by 40 minutes.
constraints: Twilio account messaging quota is 200 msg/sec across the whole account
18 worker replicas, each concurrency 20
Rate limit must be enforced globally across replicas, not per-process
Reminders are time-sensitive (sent 24h before appointment)
Must degrade gracefully: queue, don't drop, when over quota

before_state.description: Each replica sends as fast as it can with no global rate limit, so aggregate send rate vastly exceeds the account quota and triggers throttling.

issues: No global rate limit, so aggregate rate exceeds the 200/sec account quota
Immediate fixed backoff on 429 re-bursts the API
Account-level throttle blocks unrelated 2FA traffic

reasoning: Naive fix: set BullMQ limiter max:200 per worker — rejected because the limiter is per-Worker-instance, so 18 replicas each allow 200/sec = 3600/sec aggregate. Alternative: route all SMS through a single dedicated worker pod — rejected as a throughput bottleneck and single point of failure. Chosen path: a Redis-backed global token bucket that all replicas draw from before each send, refilled at 200/sec. Cross-layer trace: each replica calls a Lua token-bucket SCRIPT atomically in Redis before sending (data); if no token, the worker rate-limits itself and the job is retried shortly (async); the bucket refills at the account quota so aggregate never exceeds it (config); 429s still back off with jitter as a backstop (async); tests assert global rate is capped under multi-replica load.
