after_state.description: A Redis Lua token bucket enforces the 200/sec account quota across all replicas; workers wait for a token instead of bursting.

files.content:
const SCRIPT = `
local key = KEYS[1]
local rate = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local cap = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])
local tokens = tonumber(redis.call('hget', key, 'tokens') or cap)
local ts = tonumber(redis.call('hget', key, 'ts') or now)
tokens = math.min(cap, tokens + (now - ts) * rate)
if tokens < 1 then
  redis.call('hset', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'ts', now)
  return 0
end
redis.call('hset', key, 'tokens', tokens - 1, 'ts', now)
return 1`;
export async function takeToken(redis: Redis) {
  return redis.eval(SCRIPT, 1, 'tb:twilio', '200', '200', String(Date.now() / 1000));
}
files.purpose: Atomic Redis token bucket refilled at 200/sec shared by all replicas
Acquires a global token before each send and backs off cleanly on residual 429s
Asserts the global rate stays at/under the account quota across multiple replicas

migration_strategy: Deploy the token bucket in shadow: take tokens but don't block (count would-be-throttles)
Verify the would-be-throttle rate matches expected over-quota volume
Enable enforcement on 2 replicas; confirm aggregate Twilio 429 rate drops
Roll enforcement to all 18 replicas; alert on token starvation duration
Lower fixed backoff and remove the old immediate-retry path
testing_strategy: Fixture simulates 5 concurrent drainers pulling from one bucket for 2s and asserts the max sends in any one-second window is <=210 (side-effect on rate). Assert every reminder is eventually sent, none dropped (output). Chaos test: make Twilio return 429 despite tokens (simulating account-level throttle) and assert the worker backs off with jitter and requeues rather than hot-looping, and total throughput self-limits.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A per-Worker BullMQ limiter caps each replica independently, so 18 replicas at 200/sec each send up to 3,600/sec aggregate, 18x the Twilio account quota; the burst trips Twilio's account throttle, which then delays unrelated transactional SMS like 2FA.

title: Fix a delayed-job timezone bug that fires reminder notifications at the wrong local time after DST
business_context: A productivity app schedules reminder notifications using BullMQ delayed jobs computed from a fixed UTC offset stored at creation time. After the spring DST transition, every recurring reminder fired one hour off for 1.1M users in DST-observing regions; users missed meetings and the app's store rating dropped 0.4 stars in a week.
constraints: 12M scheduled reminders across 400 timezones
Reminders are recurring (daily/weekly) and must honor the user's local wall-clock time
DST transitions must not shift existing reminders
Rescheduling must be incremental, not a 12M-job rebuild at once
Stored schedule must survive timezone-rule (tzdata) updates

before_state.description: The next fire time is precomputed as a UTC timestamp using a frozen offset, so DST changes make wall-clock-based reminders fire an hour off.

issues: Frozen UTC offset ignores DST so reminders drift by an hour
Recomputing delay from a stale offset compounds the error each cycle
No re-anchoring to the IANA zone's current rules

reasoning: Naive fix: add an hour during DST months — rejected because DST rules differ per zone and change over time, so hardcoding is wrong globally. Alternative: store reminders as cron expressions with a tz and use BullMQ repeatable jobs with the tz option — strong, but BullMQ's repeatable tz handling alone doesn't cover ad-hoc one-off reschedules and per-user wall times for all 400 zones; we adopt its tz semantics but compute fire times with a proper tz library. Chosen path: store the IANA zone name and local wall-clock time, compute each next fire with luxon against current tzdata, and reschedule the next occurrence at fire time. Cross-layer trace: schedule stores zone + local time, not an offset (data); at each fire, the worker computes the next occurrence using DateTime.fromObject with the zone so DST is applied from live rules (data); the delay is derived from that zoned time (async); a nightly job re-anchors near-future reminders after tzdata updates (async); tests assert correct firing across a DST boundary.

after_state.description: Reminders store an IANA zone and local wall-clock time; each next fire is computed against live tz rules so DST is always correct.

files.purpose: Computes the next fire from the user's IANA zone and wall time using live DST rules
Fires then reschedules using the DST-correct computation each cycle

files.content:
ALTER TABLE reminders
  ADD COLUMN iana_zone text,
  ADD COLUMN local_hour smallint,
  ADD COLUMN local_minute smallint,
  ADD COLUMN cadence_days smallint NOT NULL DEFAULT 1;
-- backfill iana_zone from stored offset-region mapping
files.purpose: Stores zone + wall-clock time instead of a frozen offset
Asserts local-time correctness on both sides of a DST transition

migration_strategy: Add zone columns; backfill iana_zone from the existing offset-to-region mapping where unambiguous
Deploy computeNextFireUtc behind a TZ_FIX flag, used only for reminders that have iana_zone
Reschedule incrementally: as each reminder next fires, scheduleNext re-anchors it correctly (no mass rebuild)
Run a nightly re-anchor job for reminders due within 48h after a tzdata package update
Once all reminders carry iana_zone, remove the frozen-offset path
testing_strategy: Fixture sets a 09:00 local daily reminder in America/New_York and asserts the computed UTC fire maps back to 09:00 local both before and after the spring DST date (output). Side-effect: after firing, assert a new delayed job is enqueued with the correct next delay. Chaos test: simulate a tzdata update that changes a zone's DST rule and run the re-anchor job; assert affected near-future reminders shift to the new-correct local time without touching far-future ones.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Freezing utcOffsetMinutes at creation bakes in the offset that was current then; when DST flips, the real offset changes by an hour but the stored value doesn't, so the precomputed UTC fire time corresponds to the wrong wall-clock hour for every DST-observing user.

title: Add exactly-once outbox publishing so domain events aren't lost or duplicated between Postgres and Kafka
business_context: A fintech ledger service writes a transaction to Postgres then publishes a 'transaction.posted' event to Kafka in the same request. When the process crashed after the DB commit but before the Kafka publish, downstream fraud-scoring never saw 7,200 transactions; conversely a retry that published twice double-scored others. Reconciliation gaps triggered a regulatory finding.
constraints: Postgres is the system of record; Kafka feeds 6 downstream consumers
No event may be lost (regulatory) and consumers must dedupe duplicates
Throughput: 9,000 transactions/sec
Publishing must survive Kafka being briefly unavailable
No dual-write across systems without atomicity

before_state.description: The DB write and Kafka publish are separate non-atomic operations, so a crash between them loses events and a retry can double-publish.

issues: DB commit and Kafka publish are not atomic (dual-write problem)
Crash after commit loses the event
Retry after partial failure can publish twice

reasoning: Naive fix: publish to Kafka first, then write the DB — rejected because then a DB failure leaves a published event with no committed record, worse for a ledger. Alternative: Kafka transactions spanning the DB — rejected because Kafka transactions don't include the Postgres write; they can't make a cross-system commit atomic. Chosen path: the transactional outbox pattern: insert the event into an outbox table inside the SAME DB transaction as the ledger write, then a relay (CDC or polling) publishes outbox rows to Kafka and marks them sent. Cross-layer trace: postTransaction writes ledger AND outbox row atomically (data); a relay polls unsent outbox rows with SKIP LOCKED, publishes to Kafka with the row id as the message key, and marks sent only after broker ack (async); consumers dedupe on the outbox id (data); the outbox guarantees at-least-once and consumers make it effectively-once; tests assert no loss on crash and dedupe.

after_state.description: Events are written to an outbox in the same DB transaction as the ledger write; a relay reliably publishes them to Kafka, giving at-least-once with consumer dedupe.

files.purpose: Writes the ledger row and the outbox event atomically, eliminating the dual-write gap
Relays outbox rows to Kafka with SKIP LOCKED and marks them sent only after publish
Durable outbox with a partial index for cheap relay polling
Asserts an event survives a crash and is published exactly once by the relay

migration_strategy: Add the outbox table and write to it in the same transaction (events accumulate as pending, no publish yet)
Deploy the relay in shadow, publishing to a parallel topic, and verify event counts match ledger writes
Cut downstream consumers to the relayed topic; have them dedupe on the message key
Remove the inline kafka.publish dual-write from postTransaction
Add alerting on outbox backlog age to catch relay stalls
testing_strategy: Fixture posts a transaction and asserts one ledger row and one pending outbox row in the same transaction (output + side-effect). Run the relay and assert exactly one Kafka publish and the outbox row marked sent. Chaos test: kill the process after the DB commit but before relay; restart and assert the relay publishes the pending event exactly once (running relay twice does not double-publish), proving no loss and no duplication.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Writing the DB then publishing to Kafka as two separate operations is a dual-write with no atomicity: a crash in the gap commits the ledger row but loses the event forever, and a retry of the whole operation after a partial failure republishes events already sent, double-scoring downstream.

title: Fix a worker memory leak from accumulating job listeners that OOMs long-running BullMQ processes
business_context: A SaaS notification worker registered a new event listener and a new DB connection per job. Over a 36-hour uptime, listener count grew into the tens of thousands and heap climbed steadily; the worker OOMed every ~40 hours, dropping in-flight notifications and paging on-call nightly. The leak was invisible in short-lived test runs.
constraints: Workers are long-lived (days) processing ~2M jobs/day
Node heap limit is 1.5GB
Must not regress throughput; per-job overhead matters at 2M/day
Fix must be observable (heap/listener metrics) to prove resolution
No restart-every-hour band-aid allowed by SRE

before_state.description: Each job attaches a new EventEmitter listener and opens a new DB connection without cleanup, so resources accumulate until OOM.

issues: bus.on adds a listener per job and never removes it (MaxListeners warning then leak)
A fresh DB connection per job is never closed, leaking sockets and memory
Leak only manifests over long uptime, invisible in CI

reasoning: Naive fix: call bus.setMaxListeners(Infinity) to silence the warning — rejected because it hides the leak rather than fixing it; listeners still accumulate. Alternative: restart the worker hourly via a cron — rejected by SRE as it drops in-flight jobs and masks the root cause. Chosen path: hoist shared resources (one emitter, a pooled DB connection) out of the per-job closure, and use once/removeListener for any per-job subscription with guaranteed cleanup in finally. Cross-layer trace: a module-level connection pool replaces per-job connections (data); per-job event subscriptions use once or are removed in a finally block (async); a leak guard asserts listener count and pool size stay bounded (observability); the worker handler becomes allocation-light; tests run many jobs and assert stable heap and listener count.

after_state.description: Shared pooled resources and disciplined listener lifecycle keep heap and handle counts flat over long uptime.

files.purpose: Uses a shared pool and once/removeListener with finally cleanup to eliminate per-job leaks
One bounded connection pool replaces per-job connection creation
Single shared emitter with a bounded listener ceiling for leak detection
Asserts listeners are cleaned up and heap stays bounded across many jobs

migration_strategy: Add the shared pool and bus modules; refactor the handler to use them behind a build with leak metrics
Run a 24h soak in staging at production job rate and confirm flat heap and zero residual listeners
Deploy to one canary worker; compare heap-growth slope to baseline over 24h
Roll to all workers; add alerts on listener count and pool saturation
Remove any temporary hourly-restart cron that was masking the issue
testing_strategy: Fixture processes 10,000 jobs and asserts the 'delivered' listener count returns to 0 (side-effect) and heap growth is bounded after GC (output). Assert the pool never exceeds max connections (side-effect). Chaos test: force deliver() to throw for half the jobs and assert listeners are still removed and connections released via the finally block, proving the cleanup holds on the error path.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding a listener and opening a DB connection per job without removal means each of 2M daily jobs leaves a listener and a socket behind; over 36 hours that is millions of retained closures and handles, so heap climbs monotonically until the 1.5GB limit triggers OOM, and short CI runs never accumulate enough to reveal it.

title: Replace polling-based job status with a pub/sub completion signal to cut Redis load and status latency
business_context: A CI/CD platform shows live build status. The frontend polled a /status endpoint every 2s per active build; with 30,000 concurrent builds that was 15,000 req/s hammering Redis with GET-per-build, Redis CPU sat at 85%, and users still saw up-to-2s-stale status. Adding more builds risked toppling the shared Redis.
constraints: Up to 50,000 concurrent builds at peak
Status updates must reach the UI in under 500ms
Cannot scale Redis vertically much further (already large)
Must support reconnect/resume so a dropped client doesn't miss the terminal event
Frontend is a React SPA behind a CDN

before_state.description: The UI polls a status endpoint per build every 2s, generating constant Redis GETs and still showing stale state.

issues: Per-build polling creates 15k+ req/s of Redis GETs
Up to 2s stale because of the poll interval
Load scales linearly with concurrent builds, threatening Redis

reasoning: Naive fix: cache status in the app layer with a short TTL — rejected because it adds staleness and still polls. Alternative: WebSockets with per-build subscriptions — strong but heavier to operate behind the CDN and needs sticky sessions; rejected for this iteration in favor of SSE which works over plain HTTP and reconnects natively. Chosen path: the worker publishes status changes to a Redis pub/sub channel per build; the API exposes an SSE stream that subscribes and pushes events, with a last-known snapshot for resume. Cross-layer trace: the worker PUBLISHes on build:{id}:events when status changes and also SETs a snapshot key (data); the API SSE endpoint reads the snapshot then SUBSCRIBEs and streams events (API/async); the SPA opens an EventSource and updates live, using Last-Event-ID to resume (UI); load drops from per-poll GETs to one subscription per viewer; tests assert sub-500ms push and resume.

after_state.description: Workers publish status changes to Redis pub/sub; the API streams them via SSE with a snapshot for resume, cutting Redis load and latency.

files.purpose: SSE endpoint that sends a snapshot then streams live pub/sub events with resume support
Persists a snapshot and publishes each status change exactly once
React hook that subscribes via EventSource and updates status live, auto-reconnecting
Asserts a status change reaches the SSE client within the latency budget

migration_strategy: Add snapshot-set + publish in the worker alongside existing polling (no client change yet)
Ship the SSE endpoint; verify events fire and snapshots resume correctly
Update the SPA to use EventSource behind a SSE_STATUS flag for a fraction of users
Ramp to all users; watch Redis GET rate and CPU drop
Remove the polling endpoint and the per-build GET path
testing_strategy: Fixture opens an SSE stream and calls setStatus; assert the client receives the event in under 500ms (output) and that a snapshot key was set (side-effect). Assert Redis publish count equals status changes (no duplicate pushes). Chaos test: drop and reopen the SSE connection mid-build; assert the reconnect receives the current snapshot first so the client never misses the terminal 'succeeded'/'failed' event.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-build polling issues a Redis GET per build per interval, so load is O(builds / interval); at 30k builds every 2s that is 15k GET/s and grows linearly, pushing shared Redis CPU to 85% while still delivering status up to 2s stale because the client only learns of changes on its next poll.

title: Add consumer-lag-based autoscaling so a Kafka order pipeline scales out before lag breaches SLA
business_context: An e-commerce order-events pipeline (Python, aiokafka) scaled workers on CPU. During flash sales, message volume spiked but CPU stayed moderate (I/O-bound), so the HPA never scaled out; consumer lag grew to 2.1M and order confirmations were delayed 25 minutes, hurting conversion on the highest-traffic day of the year.
constraints: Topic has 60 partitions; consumers are I/O-bound (DB + payment calls)
Must scale on consumer lag, not CPU
Cannot exceed 60 consumer instances (partition count caps parallelism)
Scale decisions must be stable (no flapping)
Lag must stay under 100k to meet the 2-minute confirmation SLA

before_state.description: Autoscaling keys on CPU, which stays low for I/O-bound consumers, so the pool never scales out under lag.

files.content:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
spec:
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target: { type: Utilization, averageUtilization: 70 }  # BUG: I/O-bound, CPU never high
  minReplicas: 4
  maxReplicas: 60
issues: Scales on CPU which stays low for an I/O-bound consumer
Never reacts to growing lag
No upper bound aligned to partition count (over/under provision)

reasoning: Naive fix: lower the CPU target to 30% — rejected because CPU is simply not correlated with lag for an I/O-bound consumer; it would scale randomly. Alternative: a fixed time-based scale-up before known sales — rejected because unplanned spikes still go unhandled and it wastes capacity off-peak. Chosen path: export Kafka consumer-group lag as a custom metric and drive the HPA on lag-per-replica, capped at partition count, with stabilization to avoid flapping. Cross-layer trace: a lag exporter reads the consumer group's total lag and publishes it to Prometheus (observability); a KEDA ScaledObject (or custom-metrics HPA) targets lag-per-replica (config); maxReplicas equals partition count so we never over-provision idle consumers (config); scale-down stabilization prevents flapping (config); tests assert scale-out under synthetic lag.

after_state.description: The pool scales on consumer-group lag via KEDA, bounded by partition count and stabilized against flapping.

files.content:
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
spec:
  scaleTargetRef: { name: order-consumer }
  minReplicaCount: 4
  maxReplicaCount: 60   # == partition count; more pods would idle
  cooldownPeriod: 120   # stabilize scale-down
  triggers:
  - type: kafka
    metadata:
      bootstrapServers: kafka:9092
      consumerGroup: orders
      topic: order-events
      lagThreshold: '20000'   # scale so lag/replica stays low
      activationLagThreshold: '1000'
files.purpose: Scales the consumer deployment on Kafka lag, capped at partition count, with stabilization

files.content:
async def consume():
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer('order-events', group_id='orders', enable_auto_commit=False, max_poll_records=200)
    await consumer.start()
    async for batch in poll_batches(consumer):
        await asyncio.gather(*(handle(m) for m in batch))  # I/O concurrency within a pod
        await consumer.commit()
files.purpose: I/O-concurrent batch handling so each replica drains efficiently while KEDA adds replicas under lag

files.content:
groups:
- name: order-lag
  rules:
  - alert: OrderConsumerLagHigh
    expr: kafka_consumergroup_lag{group="orders"} > 100000
    for: 2m
    labels: { severity: page }
files.purpose: Pages if lag exceeds the SLA threshold despite autoscaling

files.content:
def test_lag_drives_replicas(fake_keda):
    fake_keda.set_lag(500_000)
    desired = fake_keda.compute_desired_replicas(lag_threshold=20_000, max_replicas=60)
    assert desired == 25  # 500k / 20k = 25, under the 60 cap
    fake_keda.set_lag(0)
    assert fake_keda.compute_desired_replicas(20_000, 60) == fake_keda.min_replicas
files.purpose: Asserts replica count tracks lag and respects the partition-count cap

migration_strategy: Deploy the Kafka lag exporter and verify the metric matches the broker's reported group lag
Install KEDA and create the ScaledObject in shadow (observe desired replicas without acting)
Compare KEDA's desired replicas to manual judgment during a load test
Switch the deployment's scaler from CPU HPA to the KEDA ScaledObject
Tune lagThreshold and cooldown from a real flash-sale rehearsal
testing_strategy: Fixture feeds a synthetic lag value and asserts the computed desired replica count equals lag/threshold capped at 60 (output). Side-effect: assert scale-down only happens after the cooldown window. Chaos test: oscillate lag rapidly and assert replicas do not flap (stabilization holds), and push lag above 100k to assert the page alert fires while replicas are already at max.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A CPU-target HPA only scales when CPU is high, but an I/O-bound consumer spends its time awaiting DB and payment calls, so CPU stays moderate even as lag explodes; the HPA sees no pressure and never adds replicas, letting lag grow to millions during the sale.

title: Replace a naive job-priority hack with a proper delayed-retry-with-DLQ flow for a fraud-scoring queue
business_context: A payments fraud-scoring worker calls an ML model service. To 'handle' transient model errors, an engineer re-added failed jobs to the front of the queue with high priority; under a model deployment blip, thousands of failing jobs kept jumping the line ahead of fresh transactions, so NEW transactions weren't scored and risky payments went through unscored for 12 minutes.
constraints: Fresh transactions must be scored within 3 seconds (p95)
Transient model errors should retry, but not at the expense of new work
After N failures a transaction must be quarantined for manual review, not retried forever
Model service has a 95% availability target; brief blips are expected
Throughput: 12,000 scoring requests/min

before_state.description: Failed jobs are re-enqueued with top priority, so a wave of failures starves fresh transactions.

issues: Failed jobs jump ahead of fresh transactions via priority 1
Retry is immediate, re-hitting a still-broken model
No cap on retries; failures loop and starve new work
No DLQ for genuinely unscoreable transactions

reasoning: Naive fix: re-add at LOW priority instead — rejected because immediate low-priority retries still hammer a broken model and never quarantine. Alternative: drop failed jobs entirely — rejected because an unscored payment is a fraud risk that must be reviewed. Chosen path: use BullMQ's native attempts + delayed exponential backoff so retries are paced and never priority-jump, then route exhausted jobs to a manual-review DLQ. Cross-layer trace: the worker lets the error propagate so BullMQ schedules a delayed retry rather than a front-of-line re-add (async); attempts and backoff bound retries (config); a failed-listener moves exhausted jobs to a review DLQ with the tx and last error (data); fresh transactions keep their natural position (async); tests assert fresh work isn't starved and exhausted jobs quarantine.

after_state.description: Failures use BullMQ delayed retries that never jump the line; exhausted jobs go to a manual-review DLQ, so fresh transactions are scored on time.

files.purpose: Relies on delayed retry/backoff so retries are paced behind fresh work, not ahead of it
Routes transactions that exhaust retries to a manual-review queue instead of looping
Persists quarantined transactions for human risk review and alerts the team
Asserts a failing job's retry does not jump ahead of fresh transactions

migration_strategy: Add the manual-review queue and worker; deploy with the failed->DLQ listener (no behavior change for successes)
Switch enqueueScore to attempts+backoff and remove the priority re-add in the same deploy
Canary on 5% of scoring traffic; inject a brief model outage and confirm fresh transactions still score on time
Roll to 100%; verify exhausted jobs land in manual-review, not an infinite loop
Remove the old priority re-enqueue code
testing_strategy: Fixture enqueues an old (failing) and a fresh tx; assert the fresh tx is scored without waiting behind the old one's retries (output ordering) and that the old tx's retry is delayed (side-effect: it reappears after a backoff delay). Chaos test: hold the model down past all attempts and assert the tx lands in the manual-review queue with its last error, and the scoring queue does not loop it forever.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Re-adding failed jobs at priority 1 puts every failure at the head of the queue; during a model blip the failing jobs continuously re-insert ahead of fresh transactions, so the worker spends all its slots retrying broken jobs while new payments wait unscored.

title: Introduce a worker-side dedupe window to absorb duplicate events from an at-least-once analytics pipeline
business_context: A product-analytics service ingests page-view events through Kafka into ClickHouse via a worker. The upstream SDK retries on network flakiness, producing duplicate events; with no dedupe, daily active user and funnel counts were inflated 8-14%, and a customer caught the discrepancy against their own logs, threatening to churn over data trust.
constraints: ~2M events/sec at peak; dedupe must be cheap
Each event has a client-generated event_id (UUID)
Duplicates arrive within seconds but occasionally minutes apart on retry
ClickHouse inserts are batched; cannot do per-row unique constraints at this rate
Dedupe state must be bounded in memory/Redis (can't keep all event_ids forever)

before_state.description: The worker inserts every event into ClickHouse with no dedupe, so SDK retries inflate counts.

issues: No dedupe, so SDK retry duplicates are double-counted
Per-row unique constraints are infeasible at 2M/sec
Counts (DAU, funnel) are inflated 8-14%

reasoning: Naive fix: a ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree to dedupe on merge — useful but merges are asynchronous and not guaranteed before a query, so live dashboards still double-count until merge. Alternative: a Postgres unique table of event_ids — rejected because 2M inserts/sec would crush Postgres. Chosen path: a bounded probabilistic + exact dedupe at ingest using a Redis-backed sliding window keyed by event_id with a TTL covering the retry window, plus ReplacingMergeTree as a long-tail backstop. Cross-layer trace: the worker checks/sets each event_id in Redis with a short TTL using a pipelined SET NX (data); first-seen events are inserted, duplicates dropped (async); the TTL bounds memory to the retry window (config); ClickHouse uses ReplacingMergeTree on event_id to catch duplicates arriving after TTL (data); tests assert duplicate suppression and bounded state.

after_state.description: A TTL-bounded Redis dedupe window drops in-window duplicates at ingest, with ReplacingMergeTree catching the rare late duplicate.

files.purpose: Pipelined SET NX dedupe drops in-window duplicates before insert, cheaply

files.content:
CREATE TABLE page_views (
  event_id UUID,
  user_id String,
  ts DateTime,
  url String
) ENGINE = ReplacingMergeTree(ts)
ORDER BY (event_id);
files.purpose: ReplacingMergeTree backstops late duplicates that arrived after the Redis TTL
Queries use FINAL so counts are correct even before background merges complete
Asserts a duplicate event within the window is inserted only once

migration_strategy: Create the ReplacingMergeTree table; deploy queries using FINAL on a copy of the dashboard
Deploy the Redis dedupe in count-only mode (log would-drop, still insert) to size the duplicate rate
Enable dedupe enforcement; compare DAU/funnel counts to the customer's logs
Tune the TTL to cover the observed retry tail (e.g., 10 min)
Remove the non-deduped insert path
testing_strategy: Fixture sends an event twice within the TTL and asserts ClickHouse receives one row (output) and dedup_dropped increments by 1 (side-effect). Assert Redis key count stays bounded by the TTL. Chaos test: send a duplicate AFTER the Redis TTL expires (simulating a late retry); assert the ReplacingMergeTree + FINAL query still reports a single distinct event, proving the backstop catches the long tail.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Inserting every event blindly means the SDK's network-retry duplicates each become a real row; aggregate metrics like DAU and funnel conversion count those duplicates, inflating numbers 8-14% and breaking customer trust when their own logs disagree.

title: Fix a deadlock between two workers updating the same rows in different order during batch processing
business_context: A logistics billing service runs two BullMQ workers: one applies shipment charges, another applies account credits. Both update the accounts and ledger_entries tables but in opposite lock order. Under concurrency they deadlocked; Postgres killed one transaction with a deadlock error ~300 times/hour, those jobs failed and retried, and nightly billing took 4x longer with sporadic missing charges.
constraints: Two job types update accounts and ledger_entries with overlapping rows
Postgres 14; deadlock detection aborts one transaction per cycle
Billing correctness: every charge and credit must be applied exactly once
Throughput must handle 500k billing ops/night within a 3-hour window
Cannot move billing off Postgres

before_state.description: The two workers lock accounts and ledger_entries in opposite orders, so concurrent runs deadlock.

issues: Locks accounts before ledger_entries
Concurrent with the credit worker's opposite order -> deadlock
Deadlock victims fail and retry, slowing billing 4x
Opposite lock order from the charge worker
Forms the deadlock cycle under concurrency
Retries on deadlock add load and reordering risk

reasoning: Naive fix: add retry-on-deadlock — rejected as it only papers over the cycle, wasting work and still slowing billing. Alternative: serialize all billing through a single worker with concurrency 1 — rejected because it cannot finish 500k ops in the 3-hour window. Chosen path: enforce a single, consistent lock ordering across both workers (always touch accounts before ledger_entries) and reduce lock footprint by partitioning work per account so the same account is never processed by two workers at once. Cross-layer trace: both workers acquire row locks in a canonical order (accounts then ledger) (data); jobs are grouped by accountId so a given account is handled by one worker at a time, removing cross-worker contention (config/async); a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on accounts establishes the lock deterministically (data); tests assert no deadlocks under concurrency.

after_state.description: Both workers lock in a single canonical order and per-account grouping ensures one account is processed at a time, eliminating the deadlock cycle.

files.purpose: Single shared function enforces the same lock order for charges and credits
Unifies both job types onto one queue with per-account grouping to remove cross-worker contention
Asserts concurrent opposite-direction ops on one account complete without deadlock

migration_strategy: Extract applyDelta and switch both workers to call it (consistent lock order) behind a LOCK_ORDER flag
Run in staging under high concurrency and confirm pg_stat shows zero deadlocks
Merge both queues into one billing queue with per-account grouping
Canary in production for one billing cycle; compare deadlock metric and runtime
Remove the old opposite-order code paths
testing_strategy: Fixture runs 200 concurrent opposite-direction ops on one account and asserts the final balance equals the deterministic net and no transaction was aborted with a deadlock error (output + side-effect on pg deadlock counter). Chaos test: drive maximum concurrency across many accounts and assert zero deadlock errors over the run, and that per-account grouping kept same-account ops serialized.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two workers locking accounts and ledger_entries in opposite orders form a classic lock-ordering cycle: worker A holds the account row and waits for the ledger lock while worker B holds the ledger and waits for the account, so Postgres detects the deadlock and aborts one transaction, and the retried victim re-enters the same cycle, ballooning billing time.

title: Add chunked checkpointing to a long-running data-migration job so a crash resumes instead of restarting
business_context: A B2B SaaS ran a one-off backfill job to recompute a denormalized field across 240M rows. The job processed in one pass with no checkpoint; at row ~180M (after 9 hours) the pod was evicted, and the job restarted from row 0, re-doing 9 hours of work and re-writing already-correct rows, ultimately taking 3 nights and blocking a feature launch.
constraints: 240M rows in Postgres; backfill must be resumable after eviction/restart
Must not re-process already-completed ranges
Backfill runs alongside live traffic; must throttle to avoid replication lag
Idempotent writes (re-running a chunk is safe but wasteful)
Must complete within a single overnight window per environment

before_state.description: The backfill scans all rows in one query and processes them in a single pass with no persisted progress.

issues: Loads/streams 240M rows with no checkpoint
A crash restarts from row 0, re-doing all prior work
No throttling, risking replication lag on the live DB

reasoning: Naive fix: log progress to a file — rejected because the file is lost when the ephemeral pod is evicted. Alternative: a Kubernetes Job with a single retry — rejected because retry still restarts from zero without persisted progress. Chosen path: keyset-chunked backfill that persists the last completed id to a checkpoint table after each chunk, throttles between chunks, and is idempotent. Cross-layer trace: a checkpoint row stores the last processed id (data); the backfill loops, reading the next chunk by keyset after the checkpoint, updating rows, then advancing and committing the checkpoint atomically (data/async); a sleep between chunks throttles to keep replication lag bounded (config); on restart it resumes from the checkpoint (async); tests assert resume-from-checkpoint and idempotency.

after_state.description: The backfill processes keyset chunks, persisting a checkpoint after each, so a restart resumes from the last completed id and throttles to protect the live DB.

files.purpose: Keyset-chunked, checkpointed, throttled backfill that resumes after a crash
Durable per-job checkpoint so progress survives pod eviction
Adaptive throttle that slows the backfill when replication lag rises
Asserts the backfill resumes from the checkpoint and does not re-process completed rows

migration_strategy: Create the checkpoint table; run the chunked backfill in a dev environment to validate resume and idempotency
Run in staging at the chunk size and throttle that keep replication lag under 5s
Run in production overnight; if evicted, restart and confirm it resumes from the checkpoint
Verify a sampled set of rows have correct denorm values
Drop the checkpoint row and remove the one-off job after completion
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds 50k records, runs until a simulated crash at id 20k, asserts the checkpoint advanced, then resumes and asserts total UPDATEs are roughly the remaining rows, not the full set (output + side-effect on update count). Chaos test: kill the process repeatedly at random chunk boundaries and assert the final state has every row's denorm correct exactly once and the checkpoint equals the max id.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single-pass backfill holds all progress only in memory, so a pod eviction at row 180M discards the fact that 180M rows were done; the restart begins again at row 0, re-doing 9 hours of work and re-writing correct rows, which is why it took three nights.

title: Split a mixed-latency queue into fast and slow lanes so quick jobs stop waiting behind 10-minute jobs
business_context: A devtools platform runs both quick lint jobs (2s) and slow full-build jobs (up to 10min) in one BullMQ queue with concurrency 8. During busy periods, all 8 slots filled with 10-minute builds, so 2-second lint jobs queued for minutes; developers saw their PR checks 'pending' for 8 minutes and complained the platform felt broken.
constraints: Job durations range from 2s to 600s in the same queue
Fast jobs must start within 5s (p95) regardless of slow-job load
Slow jobs must still get adequate capacity, not be starved
Single worker fleet; cannot 10x machine count
Job type is known at enqueue time

before_state.description: Fast and slow jobs share one queue and one concurrency pool, so slow jobs monopolize the slots and block fast ones.

issues: One pool: slow builds occupy all 8 slots
Fast lint jobs wait minutes behind slow builds
No reserved capacity for low-latency work

reasoning: Naive fix: raise concurrency to 32 — rejected because 32 concurrent 10-minute builds exhaust CPU/memory on the build machines and slow everything. Alternative: BullMQ priority so lints outrank builds — rejected because a build already running cannot be preempted, so a fast job still waits up to 10 minutes for a slot to free. Chosen path: separate fast and slow queues with dedicated, reserved concurrency per lane so fast jobs always have free slots. Cross-layer trace: enqueue routes by job type to fast or slow queue (config); a fast worker with reserved slots guarantees lints start immediately (async); a slow worker bounds heavy builds to a capacity the machines tolerate (config); resource usage is partitioned so neither starves the other (config); tests assert fast-lane latency under slow-lane saturation.

after_state.description: Fast and slow lanes have separate reserved concurrency, so quick jobs always have a free slot while builds run in their own bounded pool.

files.purpose: Routes jobs into a fast lane (lints/units) and a slow lane (builds) by type
Dedicated worker pools so fast and slow lanes have independent, reserved capacity
Tracks per-lane time-to-start to prove the fast-lane SLA
Asserts a lint job starts within the SLA even when the slow lane is saturated

migration_strategy: Create fast and slow queues; deploy routing dual-write behind a LANES flag (still process old queue too)
Start the two lane workers in shadow consuming the new queues
Flip LANES on so new jobs route to lanes; drain the legacy queue
Tune fast/slow concurrency from observed per-lane latency and machine load
Remove the single combined queue and worker
testing_strategy: Fixture floods the slow lane with 50 builds then enqueues a lint; assert the lint starts within 5s (output) while builds continue in their bounded pool (side-effect: slow lane keeps 6 active). Chaos test: saturate both lanes simultaneously and assert fast-lane p95 start latency stays under SLA, proving reserved capacity rather than shared contention.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With one queue and a shared concurrency of 8, eight in-progress 10-minute builds occupy every slot; BullMQ won't free a slot until a build finishes, so a 2-second lint enqueued behind them waits up to 10 minutes despite being trivial work.

title: Make a Kafka rebalance safe so in-flight messages aren't double-processed during consumer group scaling
business_context: A social platform's notification fan-out consumer auto-scaled during traffic spikes. Each scale event triggered a Kafka rebalance; because the consumer auto-committed and didn't handle partition revocation, in-flight messages on revoked partitions were reprocessed by the new owner, sending 220k duplicate push notifications in one scaling event and prompting user complaints about notification spam.
constraints: Consumer group scales 5-40 instances based on load
Rebalances happen on every scale and on pod restarts
Notifications must be effectively-once (duplicates are user-visible)
Cooperative rebalancing should minimize stop-the-world pauses
Must commit progress precisely at partition revocation

before_state.description: Auto-commit plus no revocation handling means a rebalance reassigns partitions mid-batch and the new owner reprocesses uncommitted-but-handled messages.

issues: Auto-commit not aligned to revocation, so handled offsets can be lost on rebalance
No revocation hook to commit before partitions move
No idempotency, so reprocessing sends duplicate pushes

reasoning: Naive fix: shorten auto-commit interval — rejected because it only narrows but doesn't close the revocation gap. Alternative: rely on Kafka EOS transactions — rejected because the sink is a push-notification API, not Kafka, so EOS can't span it. Chosen path: cooperative-sticky rebalancing, manual commit, a revocation handler that commits processed offsets before partitions are reassigned, and an idempotency guard on push delivery. Cross-layer trace: consumer uses cooperative-sticky assignor to avoid stop-the-world reassignment (config); manual commit after each handled message or batch (async); a partitionsRevoked callback commits processed offsets synchronously before yielding the partition (async); pushNotification dedupes on notification_id in Redis (data); tests assert no duplicate push across a forced rebalance.

after_state.description: Cooperative rebalancing with revocation-time commits and an idempotent push guard ensures messages aren't reprocessed across scaling events.

files.purpose: Cooperative rebalancing with manual commit and a commit at revocation to avoid reprocessing
Dedupes push delivery so any residual reprocessing is harmless
Asserts no duplicate notifications are sent across a scaling rebalance

migration_strategy: Add the Redis idempotency guard to pushNotification (safe with the old consumer too)
Deploy the cooperative-sticky, manual-commit consumer to a canary instance in the group
Trigger a controlled scale event and verify zero duplicate pushes and minimal pause
Roll to all instances; monitor duplicate-push metric during scaling
Remove auto-commit configuration
testing_strategy: Fixture produces 1,000 notifications and runs the consumer while forcing a rebalance (scale up); assert each notification id is delivered exactly once (output) and offsets were committed at revocation (side-effect). Chaos test: kill an instance mid-batch to force an abrupt rebalance and assert the idempotency guard prevents any duplicate push even though some offsets were uncommitted at the crash.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Timer-based auto-commit means at a rebalance, partitions move to a new owner while some already-handled messages have not had their offsets committed; the new owner re-reads those offsets and re-sends the notifications, producing a burst of duplicates exactly when scaling triggers a rebalance.

title: Add per-job timeouts and cancellation to stop a hung external call from pinning a worker slot forever
business_context: A B2B integration platform syncs data via customer-provided webhook endpoints. One customer's endpoint accepted the TCP connection but never responded; with no request timeout, the fetch hung indefinitely, pinning a worker slot. Over a day, 6 such hung endpoints consumed 6 of 8 slots, throughput collapsed, and unrelated customers' syncs stalled for hours.
constraints: Worker concurrency is 8 per replica; a pinned slot is a real capacity loss
External endpoints are untrusted and may hang, slow-loris, or never respond
A hung job must be aborted and retried/failed, not held forever
Cancellation must release sockets, not just abandon the promise
Sync SLA is 5 minutes per job

before_state.description: The handler awaits an external fetch with no timeout, so a non-responding endpoint hangs the job and occupies its slot indefinitely.

issues: No request timeout; a hung endpoint never returns
The awaited promise never settles, pinning the worker slot
No cancellation/abort to release the socket

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the fetch in Promise.race with a timeout — rejected alone because racing only abandons the promise; the underlying socket stays open and the connection leaks. Alternative: set a global BullMQ stalled timeout to kill the job — rejected because the worker process stays blocked on the open socket and the stalled job is reclaimed elsewhere only to hang again. Chosen path: AbortController-based timeout that actually cancels the fetch and closes the socket, plus a hard job-level deadline. Cross-layer trace: each fetch gets an AbortSignal.timeout so the socket is closed on timeout (async); a job-level deadline wraps the whole handler so even non-fetch hangs are bounded (async); timed-out jobs throw a retryable error and free the slot (async); a per-endpoint failure counter trips a circuit so a chronically-hung endpoint is skipped (data); tests assert the slot frees and the socket closes.

after_state.description: Fetches use an abortable timeout that closes the socket, and a job deadline bounds the whole handler, so a hung endpoint frees its slot promptly.

files.purpose: Abortable fetch timeout plus a hard job deadline so a hung endpoint never pins a slot
Bounds total handler time so even non-fetch hangs are caught and the slot is freed
Skips a chronically-unresponsive endpoint to protect overall throughput
Asserts a hung endpoint times out, frees the slot, and closes the socket

migration_strategy: Deploy abortable fetch timeouts and the job deadline behind a TIMEOUTS flag, default on for new jobs
Canary one replica; point a test at a hanging server and confirm the slot frees within the deadline
Roll to all replicas; add the per-endpoint timeout breaker
Tune timeout values from real endpoint latency percentiles
Remove the unbounded fetch path
testing_strategy: Fixture points the worker at a server that accepts the connection but never responds; assert the handler rejects within the 30s fetch timeout (output), the slot is freed, and the test server reports zero lingering open sockets (side-effect). Chaos test: run 8 concurrent jobs all targeting hanging endpoints and assert all 8 slots recover within the deadline rather than staying pinned, restoring throughput.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A plain await fetch with no timeout returns only when the endpoint responds; an endpoint that accepts the connection but never replies leaves the promise unsettled forever, so the worker slot is occupied indefinitely, and six such endpoints permanently consume six of eight slots.

title: Replace a fan-in race with a counter-based completion barrier so a parent job fires once all children finish
business_context: A media platform transcodes a video into 6 renditions as 6 child jobs, then a parent 'publish' job assembles a manifest once all renditions exist. The completion check polled S3 for all 6 files; due to eventual consistency and a race, publish sometimes ran with only 5 renditions present, shipping broken adaptive-bitrate streams to viewers for ~3% of uploads.
constraints: 6 child rendition jobs per video; counts can vary by source
Children can complete out of order and retry independently
Parent publish must run exactly once, after the LAST child
No reliance on S3 list consistency for the completion decision
Throughput: 40k videos/day

before_state.description: The parent decides completion by listing S3, which is racy and eventually consistent, so it can run before all children are durably done.

issues: S3 list is eventually consistent, so a just-written file may be missing
Each child calls maybePublish -> race where two see >=expected and double-publish, or one sees too few
No atomic completion counter

reasoning: Naive fix: add a delay before listing — rejected because eventual consistency has no fixed bound and a delay both slows publishing and doesn't guarantee correctness. Alternative: have the parent poll a DB row count of rendition records — workable but still a poll; chosen instead is an atomic counter so the last child triggers publish deterministically. Chosen path: a Redis atomic DECR (or DB UPDATE ... RETURNING) of a per-video remaining-count, where the child that drives it to zero enqueues the publish exactly once. Cross-layer trace: when a video starts, set remaining = number of renditions (data); each child, on durable success, atomically DECRs the counter (data); the child whose DECR returns 0 enqueues the publish job with a deterministic jobId (async); publish reads the manifest from recorded rendition rows, not an S3 list (data); tests assert publish fires exactly once after the last child.

after_state.description: An atomic per-video counter triggers publish exactly once when the final child completes, independent of S3 list consistency.

files.purpose: Atomic DECR barrier: only the last child enqueues a single publish job
Initializes the completion counter to the exact rendition count before fan-out
Publishes using the durable rendition records so no rendition can be missed
Asserts the publish job is enqueued exactly once when the final child completes

migration_strategy: Add the renditions table and counter initialization; deploy DECR barrier behind a BARRIER flag (S3 poll still runs)
Compare barrier-triggered publishes to the old path; confirm they agree and never miss a rendition
Flip BARRIER on so publish triggers only via the counter; disable the S3 poll
Monitor for any video stuck with remaining>0 (indicates a failed child) and alert
Remove the S3-list completion code
testing_strategy: Fixture starts a 3-rendition transcode and processes the children in random order; assert publishQueue.add('publish') is called exactly once after the third child (output) and the manifest is built from 3 rendition rows (side-effect). Chaos test: make one child fail and retry; assert publish is NOT enqueued until the retry succeeds and drives the counter to zero, proving no early or duplicate publish.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Deciding completion by listing S3 depends on eventual consistency, so a freshly written rendition may not yet appear; the parent sees only 5 of 6 files and publishes a manifest missing a rendition, and concurrent maybePublish calls race so publish can fire early or twice.

title: Fix a worker that loses jobs by acking before a transactional DB write commits in RabbitMQ
business_context: An insurance claims intake service consumes RabbitMQ messages and writes claims to Postgres. The consumer used autoAck and a separate DB transaction; when the DB transaction failed (constraint violation or deadlock), the message was already acked and gone, silently dropping ~150 claims/day that customers had to resubmit, eroding trust and triggering complaints to a regulator.
constraints: RabbitMQ classic queue; consumers prefetch 50
DB write can fail (constraints, deadlocks) and must then redeliver
No claim may be lost; duplicates are tolerable if idempotent
Throughput: 4,000 claims/min
Poison messages must eventually DLQ, not loop forever

before_state.description: autoAck removes the message on delivery, before the DB write; a failed write loses the claim with no redelivery.

issues: noAck=true acks on delivery, before the DB write commits
A failed transaction loses the message permanently
No DLQ for poison messages; no idempotency

reasoning: Naive fix: just set noAck=false and ack at the end — necessary but insufficient because a redelivered message could be inserted twice without idempotency, and a poison message would nack-loop forever. Chosen path: manual ack after a successful commit, nack-with-requeue on transient failures, route to a DLX after a redelivery cap, and an idempotency key for safe redelivery. Cross-layer trace: consumer disables autoAck (config); on success it acks AFTER commit, on transient failure nacks with requeue, on poison (exceeded redelivery count) nacks without requeue so the dead-letter exchange routes it to a DLQ (async); the insert is idempotent on claim_id so redelivery doesn't duplicate (data); tests assert no loss on failure and DLQ for poison.

after_state.description: Messages are acked only after a successful commit; transient failures requeue, poison messages dead-letter, and inserts are idempotent.

files.purpose: Acks only after commit, requeues transient failures, dead-letters poison, and inserts idempotently
Sets up a dead-letter exchange and queue for messages that exhaust redelivery

files.content: ALTER TABLE claims ADD CONSTRAINT claims_pk PRIMARY KEY (claim_id);
files.purpose: Makes the idempotent insert work so redelivery can't duplicate a claim
Asserts a failed write requeues the message rather than acking and losing it

migration_strategy: Add the claims primary key and idempotent insert (safe with the old consumer)
Declare the dead-letter exchange/queue and set the DLX policy on the claims queue
Deploy the manual-ack consumer to one canary node; inject a DB failure and confirm requeue, not loss
Roll to all consumers; monitor the DLQ for genuine poison messages
Remove the autoAck path
testing_strategy: Fixture delivers a claim; on success assert one claims row and the message acked (output + side-effect). On a forced DB failure assert the message is nacked-with-requeue and not acked (no loss). Chaos test: keep failing a specific poison message past the redelivery cap and assert it lands in claims.dlq via the DLX rather than looping, and that a later valid redelivery of a different claim still inserts exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With noAck=true the broker considers the message delivered and discards it the instant it hands it to the consumer; if the subsequent DB transaction throws (a deadlock or constraint violation), there is no message left to redeliver, so the claim is silently and permanently lost.

title: Add a concurrency-limited fan-out so a parent job stops spawning unbounded gRPC calls and exhausting connections
business_context: A logistics platform recomputes ETAs for all stops on a route by calling a routing gRPC service, one call per stop, fired with Promise.all. A 4,000-stop mega-route fired 4,000 concurrent gRPC calls, exhausted the HTTP/2 connection's max concurrent streams, got RST_STREAM errors, and the routing service shed load, breaking ETAs for every route for 6 minutes.
constraints: Routes range from 5 to 4,000 stops
gRPC channel allows ~100 concurrent streams before backpressure
ETA recompute must finish within 20s for a 4,000-stop route
Partial failures must retry individual stops, not the whole route
Must not overwhelm the shared routing service (used by many features)

before_state.description: The handler fires one gRPC call per stop with Promise.all, so concurrency equals stop count and saturates the channel.

issues: Promise.all fires all gRPC calls at once, exceeding channel stream limits
RST_STREAM/connection exhaustion under large routes
One failed call rejects the whole Promise.all, dropping all ETAs

reasoning: Naive fix: chunk into groups of 100 with sequential awaits — better but still bursty at chunk boundaries and rigid. Alternative: push each stop as its own BullMQ job — valid but adds queue overhead and loses the natural per-route grouping and 20s budget control. Chosen path: a bounded-concurrency map (p-limit style) within the handler that caps in-flight gRPC calls and isolates per-stop failures. Cross-layer trace: the handler maps stops through a concurrency limiter set below the channel stream limit (async); each call has its own retry so one bad stop doesn't drop the route (async); results are collected with per-stop success/failure (data); the limiter keeps the routing service within its capacity (config); tests assert bounded concurrency and partial-failure isolation.

after_state.description: A bounded-concurrency limiter caps in-flight gRPC calls below the channel limit and isolates per-stop failures, so large routes complete without saturating the service.

files.purpose: Bounded fan-out with per-stop retry/isolation and a follow-up job for failed stops
Concurrency-limited map keeping in-flight calls below the gRPC stream limit
Per-call jittered retry so one transient gRPC error doesn't fail the stop
Asserts in-flight gRPC concurrency stays bounded for a huge route

migration_strategy: Deploy mapLimit at a conservative limit (32) behind a BOUNDED_FANOUT flag
Canary on routes over 500 stops; confirm RST_STREAM errors disappear and ETAs still finish in budget
Tune the limit up to 64 based on routing-service headroom
Enable for all routes; add the failed-stops follow-up queue
Remove the Promise.all fan-out
testing_strategy: Fixture runs a 4,000-stop route and asserts peak in-flight gRPC calls stays <=64 (side-effect) and all stops get an ETA saved (output, count match). Chaos test: make 5% of computeEta calls fail transiently; assert those stops are retried and any still-failing stops are enqueued for follow-up while the rest of the route's ETAs are saved, proving per-stop isolation instead of dropping the whole route.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.all over 4,000 stops issues 4,000 gRPC calls simultaneously; the HTTP/2 channel allows only ~100 concurrent streams, so the excess triggers RST_STREAM and the routing service sheds load, and because Promise.all rejects on the first failure, every ETA for the route is lost.

title: Move heavy synchronous work out of a Django request into Celery to fix gunicorn worker exhaustion
business_context: A B2B SaaS generates and emails a 50-page onboarding packet synchronously inside a Django view. The PDF render takes 8-15s; with only 12 gunicorn workers, a burst of 20 onboarding signups tied up every worker for 15s each, so the entire app (including login) returned 502s for 90 seconds during a launch webinar with 600 attendees.
constraints: 12 gunicorn sync workers; each blocked request is a lost worker
PDF render + email is 8-15s of CPU/IO
User must get immediate feedback that the packet is being prepared
Packet must be delivered reliably even if the web process restarts
Existing Celery + Redis broker is available

before_state.description: The view renders the PDF and sends the email inline, blocking a gunicorn worker for the full duration.

issues: Blocks a sync gunicorn worker for 8-15s
A burst exhausts all 12 workers -> 502s app-wide
No retry if the email send fails mid-request

reasoning: Naive fix: add more gunicorn workers — rejected because heavy 15s tasks would still saturate any fixed worker count under a burst and waste memory. Alternative: switch to async workers (gevent) — rejected because the PDF render is CPU-bound and would block the event loop, not just IO. Chosen path: enqueue a Celery task and return immediately, with the task rendering and emailing with retries and idempotency. Cross-layer trace: the view calls delay() and returns 202 with a task status (UI/API); the Celery task renders the PDF and emails, with autoretry on transient email errors (async); an idempotency guard keyed by (user, packet_version) prevents double-send on retry (data); a status endpoint lets the UI confirm delivery (API); tests assert the view returns fast and the task delivers exactly once.

after_state.description: The view enqueues a Celery task and returns immediately; the task renders and emails reliably with retries and idempotency.

files.purpose: Returns 202 instantly, freeing the gunicorn worker
Renders and emails off the request thread with retries and a dedupe guard
Tracks delivery state and enforces one send per user+packet version

files.content:
def test_view_returns_fast_and_task_sends_once(client, mocker):
    send = mocker.patch('tasks.send_email')
    resp = client.post('/onboarding/packet')
    assert resp.status_code == 202
    generate_packet.run(user.id)
    generate_packet.run(user.id)  # retry
    assert send.call_count == 1
files.purpose: Asserts the view responds 202 quickly and the task sends exactly once across retries

migration_strategy: Add the PacketDelivery model and Celery task; deploy with the view still synchronous behind ASYNC_PACKET=false
Verify the task renders and emails correctly in staging with injected SMTP failures (retry path)
Flip ASYNC_PACKET=true so the view enqueues; monitor 502 rate during a load test
Add a status endpoint and UI toast confirming delivery
Remove the synchronous render/email from the view
testing_strategy: Fixture posts to the view and asserts a 202 within milliseconds (output) without rendering inline (side-effect: no PDF call in the request). Run the task and assert one email send and one PacketDelivery row marked sent. Chaos test: make send_email raise SMTPException twice then succeed; assert the task retries with backoff and still results in exactly one delivered packet via the idempotency guard.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A synchronous view holds its gunicorn worker for the entire 8-15s render-and-email; with only 12 workers, 20 simultaneous signups occupy all of them for 15s, so every other request (including login) has no free worker and the load balancer returns 502s app-wide.

title: Add poison-message detection by attempt count so a malformed job stops infinitely retrying and DLQs
business_context: A fintech statement-generation worker hit a malformed account record that threw a parse error every time. With BullMQ attempts set very high to be 'safe', the job retried 50 times over hours, and because backoff was short, it consumed a worker slot continuously and emitted 50 PagerDuty alerts for the same failure, causing alert fatigue and a missed real incident.
constraints: Statement jobs should retry transient failures but quarantine permanent ones
A permanent parse error must DLQ after a small number of attempts
Alerting must fire once per poison message, not per attempt
Must distinguish transient (DB timeout) from permanent (parse) errors
Quarantined jobs need enough context to fix and replay

before_state.description: All errors are treated alike with a huge attempts count, so a permanent error retries dozens of times and alerts on every attempt.

issues: attempts:50 retries permanent errors dozens of times
No distinction between transient and permanent errors
Alerts fire per attempt, causing alert fatigue

reasoning: Naive fix: lower attempts to 1 — rejected because then a transient DB timeout permanently fails a real statement. Alternative: classify errors only by message strings — fragile and incomplete. Chosen path: a typed error taxonomy where permanent errors throw an UnrecoverableError that BullMQ does not retry, transient errors use bounded attempts with backoff, and alerting fires only on final failure or DLQ. Cross-layer trace: the handler classifies the error and rethrows UnrecoverableError for permanent ones so BullMQ skips retries (async); transient errors retry within a small attempts budget (config); a failed listener moves the job to a DLQ and alerts only when attemptsMade reaches the limit or the error is unrecoverable (data/observability); the DLQ carries the raw payload and error for replay (data); tests assert permanent errors don't retry and alert once.

after_state.description: Permanent errors throw UnrecoverableError and go straight to DLQ; transient errors retry within a small budget; alerts fire once per poison message.

files.purpose: Classifies permanent vs transient errors so only transient ones retry
Routes poison jobs to a DLQ and alerts exactly once on terminal failure
Replays quarantined jobs after the malformed records are corrected
Asserts a permanent error skips retries and produces one alert

migration_strategy: Introduce the error taxonomy and UnrecoverableError usage behind a CLASSIFY flag, keeping attempts at 4
Move alerting to the failed listener gated on terminal/unrecoverable conditions
Canary on a subset; inject a malformed record and confirm a single DLQ entry and one alert
Roll to all workers; verify alert volume per poison message is one
Remove the per-attempt alert and the attempts:50 config
testing_strategy: Fixture runs a job with a malformed payload and asserts it throws UnrecoverableError, is not retried (side-effect: attemptsMade stays low), lands in the DLQ with the raw payload, and triggers exactly one alert (output). Chaos test: make generateStatement fail transiently (ETIMEDOUT) twice then succeed and assert the job retries within the budget and completes without alerting, proving the transient/permanent split.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Treating every error as retryable with attempts:50 means a malformed record that throws deterministically retries 50 times, each retry occupying a worker slot and firing a separate alert; the queue makes no progress on that slot and on-call drowns in 50 identical pages for one root cause.

title: Replace a leaky scheduled-job lock with a fencing token to prevent two schedulers running the same job
business_context: A SaaS runs a singleton 'invoice sweep' job guarded by a Redis lock with a TTL. A GC pause on the lock holder exceeded the TTL; the lock expired, a second instance acquired it and started sweeping, then the first instance woke up and also wrote invoices — both ran concurrently, generating duplicate invoices for 1,800 customers and a finance reconciliation nightmare.
constraints: Exactly one invoice sweep may run at a time, cluster-wide
Lock holders can pause (GC) longer than the lock TTL
A stale lock holder must not write after losing the lock
Sweep writes to the invoices table and an external billing API
Must auto-recover if a holder genuinely dies

before_state.description: A plain TTL lock can expire under a GC pause, letting a second instance start while the first resumes and also writes, with no guard against the stale writer.

issues: TTL can expire mid-run under a GC pause
No fencing token, so a stale holder still writes after losing the lock
Two instances can run concurrently and double-invoice

reasoning: Naive fix: increase the TTL — rejected because any finite TTL can be exceeded by a long pause, and a too-long TTL delays recovery from a real crash. Alternative: a single leader-elected pod — rejected because leader election has its own split-brain windows and the writes still need protection. Chosen path: a monotonically increasing fencing token issued with each lock acquisition; the protected resource (DB/billing API) rejects writes carrying a token older than the latest seen. Cross-layer trace: lock acquisition returns an incrementing token from a Redis INCR (data); the sweep passes the token to each write; the invoices table stores the highest token and a conditional write rejects stale tokens (data); the external billing API call includes the token and the service checks it (config); a genuinely dead holder's token is simply superseded (async); tests assert a stale writer is fenced off.

after_state.description: Each lock acquisition issues a fencing token; writes carrying a stale token are rejected, so a paused-then-resumed holder cannot corrupt state.

files.purpose: Issues a monotonic fencing token with each lock acquisition
Conditional write that aborts if a newer token exists, fencing off a stale holder
Stores the highest fencing token seen so stale writes can be rejected
Asserts a resumed stale holder cannot write after a newer token advanced the fence

migration_strategy: Add the sweep_fence table and the conditional write helper; deploy alongside the existing lock (tokens issued but not yet enforced)
Enable fencing enforcement on writes behind a FENCE flag in staging; simulate a GC pause and confirm the stale writer aborts
Have the external billing API validate the fencing token
Enable FENCE in production; monitor for fenced-write events (indicates a real overlap was caught)
Remove the unfenced write path
testing_strategy: Fixture acquires a token, lets the TTL expire, has a second instance acquire a newer token and write (advancing the fence), then asserts the first instance's write is rejected with a fenced error (output + side-effect: invoices written only by the current holder). Chaos test: simulate a long GC pause on the holder and assert no duplicate invoices are created because the stale token is rejected at both the DB and billing-API boundaries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A bare TTL lock assumes the holder finishes within the TTL, but a GC pause longer than 30s lets the lock expire and a second instance acquire it; when the first instance resumes it still believes it holds the lock and writes invoices concurrently with the second, double-invoicing because nothing fences the stale writer.

title: Add bounded buffering and drop-policy to an IoT ingestion worker to survive bursts without unbounded memory growth
business_context: An IoT fleet-telemetry service buffers incoming MQTT messages in an in-memory array before batch-inserting to the database. During a firmware rollout, 200k devices reconnected and replayed buffered data simultaneously; the in-memory buffer grew without bound, the worker's heap hit the limit, and it OOM-crashed in a loop, losing the very reconnect data it was supposed to capture.
constraints: Burst rate can be 10x steady-state for minutes during fleet reconnects
DB batch insert sustains ~50k rows/sec; bursts exceed this
Memory must stay bounded; OOM is worse than shedding low-value data
Critical telemetry (alarms) must never be dropped; routine samples may be
Worker heap limit is 1GB

before_state.description: An unbounded in-memory buffer accumulates messages faster than the DB drains them, growing until OOM.

issues: Unbounded buffer.push during a burst -> heap exhaustion
No backpressure or drop policy
Critical vs routine messages treated identically

reasoning: Naive fix: increase the flush rate — rejected because the DB caps at 50k rows/sec, so above that the buffer still grows unboundedly. Alternative: block the MQTT receive when full — MQTT QoS0 can't be backpressured at the broker easily and blocking the event loop drops the connection. Chosen path: a bounded ring buffer with a priority-aware drop policy that sheds routine samples but never alarms, plus a high-water mark that pauses non-critical intake. Cross-layer trace: incoming messages classify as critical or routine (data); a bounded buffer enforces a max length, dropping the oldest routine entries when full while preserving criticals in a separate never-dropped queue (data); a drain loop batch-inserts at the DB's sustainable rate (async); metrics count drops so shedding is visible (observability); tests assert bounded memory and zero alarm loss under burst.

after_state.description: A bounded, priority-aware buffer sheds routine samples but never alarms, keeping memory flat under bursts while draining at the DB's sustainable rate.

files.purpose: Bounded buffer that drops only routine samples and always preserves criticals
Classifies and buffers messages with a memory cap, draining at the DB rate
Surfaces buffer depth and drop counts so shedding is observable
Asserts routine data is bounded and alarms are never dropped under a burst

migration_strategy: Deploy the bounded buffer with a high maxRoutine in metric-only mode (count would-drop without dropping)
Size maxRoutine from the observed steady-state-to-burst ratio and heap headroom
Enable enforcement; run a simulated fleet-reconnect burst and confirm flat memory and zero alarm loss
Add alerts on drop rate and critical-buffer depth
Remove the unbounded array path
testing_strategy: Fixture pushes 100k routine samples plus an alarm into a small buffer and asserts the routine length stays at the cap (side-effect on memory bound) and the alarm survives a drain (output). Chaos test: simulate a sustained 10x burst for 60s and assert heap stays under 1GB, the drop counter increases for routine data only, and every alarm message is eventually inserted into the DB.
what_breaks_naive_approach: An unbounded buffer.push accepts messages at the burst rate while the DB drains at a fixed 50k/sec; during a 200k-device reconnect the arrival rate far exceeds drain, so the array grows monotonically until the 1GB heap limit triggers OOM, crash-looping and losing all buffered data including alarms.
